Strawman five build slices follow: foundation (tokens module, base widget refactors, sidebar, About dialog), governance panel retrofit, methodology panel retrofit, dialog and form-control polish, closeout. PI-001's status changes from 'Open (deferred 4×)' to 'Open (active — parallel workstream)'. The version-bundling question (ship as v0.5 vs. ship as v0.6) is left for the styling workstream's Conversation 2 to settle. Boundary discipline between the two workstreams: styling owns the visual layer (QSS, design tokens, palette/typography/spacing, panel chrome, sidebar visuals, About dialog, hover/focus/disabled states); v0.5 owns the data/routing layer (config.py, ActiveEngagementContext, alembic-per-engagement, API server routing, engagement entity type, engagement-switcher mechanism). One real coupling point: v0.5's new engagement panel (slice B in the strawman) inherits whatever design tokens have shipped at the time the slice lands — either order ships fine. See PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/styling-workstream-plan.md for the full plan.
rationale: Three things changed since the four-deferral pattern was established. First, the no-production-use condition is now structural rather than incidental — v2's only user is the v2 build itself, whose friction is largely with structural decisions (schema, routing, identifiers) rather than visual treatment. Even when CBM Phase 1 starts (post-v0.5), the data shape is thin enough that visual friction will be muted. Second, the system is no longer small — v0.4 ships sixteen panels across two sidebar groups, five dialogs, two scope-level concerns; designing a visual language now is comprehensive design, not a quick polish. Third, the deferral mechanism has become an anti-pattern — each deferral added another trigger condition; none have ever fired; the open PI has become a placeholder. Either the work is real and should be done, or PI-001 should be retired. The conversation concluded the former. Parallel execution rather than v0.5-then-styling-sequential is chosen because the work is independent of v0.5's data/routing layer (see boundary discipline), scheduling certainty exceeds clean release scoping at this point, and CBM Phase 1 (when it begins post-v0.5) benefits from landing on a coherent system rather than a Qt-default one.
title: PI-001 (styling design pass) reopens as parallel workstream alongside v0.5, replacing fifth deferral

alternatives_considered: Run the paper-test against the dogfood instance, accept co-mingling. Rejected — co-mingling is exactly what v0.5 prevents; defeating v0.5's purpose in advance defeats the rationale for the v0.5 detour. Run the paper-test against a manually-spun-up second v2 instance via env-var override before v0.5 ships. Rejected — the env-var mechanism exists but no second instance has been created, no naming convention exists, and the operational debt would still be incurred without v0.5's UI / registry / switching mechanism. Move the kickoff document to a 'deferred/' subfolder. Rejected as premature; a status header is sufficient and avoids breaking any pre-existing references to the path. Edit the kickoff body to anticipate v0.5. Rejected — anticipating v0.5 before v0.5 ships invents specifics the actual Conversation 1's might revise. Light run-time editing at the start of the paper-test conversation is the cleaner approach.
consequences: The paper-test conversation does not run next. It runs after v0.5 ships and a CBM engagement record is created. The kickoff document is annotated with a Status: Deferred header that names the predecessor work; the document body remains identical. The light run-time editing required to reflect that v0.5 has shipped happens in the paper-test's actual opening conversation, not pre-emptively.
context: The paper-test conversation kickoff at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/methodology-schemas-cbm-paper-test-kickoff.md was authored 05-15-26 11:00 (commit 75f2432) and was framed as the next-up conversation after v0.4 closeout. The kickoff describes a structured validation conversation reading existing CBM domain content against the four v0.4 MVS schemas (domain, entity, process, crm_candidate) and producing two deliverables: a findings report and a single decision — 'ship CBM redo Phase 1 on v0.4 as-is' or 'amend N schemas first.' The kickoff predates v0.5 and assumes there is one v2 instance available to test against. DEC-075 inserts v0.5 between v0.4 ship and any CBM activity; running the paper-test now would land CBM domain content in the v2-build dogfood instance.
decision: **Defer the paper-test conversation until v0.5 ships and a CBM engagement record is created.** A 'Status: Deferred' blockquote header is added at the top of methodology-schemas-cbm-paper-test-kickoff.md naming the predecessor work (v0.5 ships; CBM engagement created in v2 with its own SQLite database; light run-time editing to reflect those changes). The kickoff body is unchanged because the paper-test's scope and method are unaffected — only the timing changes. The paper-test runs against the freshly-created CBM engagement, which is empty until CBM Phase 1 begins.
rationale: Running the paper-test against the dogfood instance would co-mingle CBM domain content with the v2-build's own governance content in one SQLite file with one identifier sequence — exactly the data-co-mingling problem v0.5 exists to solve (DEC-075). Deferring is the correct action; the alternative is doing the work twice (once now in the dogfood, again post-v0.5 in the proper CBM engagement) or accepting permanent co-mingling. The deferral is timing-only, not scope-or-method, so no kickoff body changes are required; a header note suffices.
title: Paper-test conversation deferred until v0.5 ships and a CBM engagement record is created

alternatives_considered: Option B — directory scan of crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements/*.db with engagement metadata stored inside each engagement's own DB. Rejected because populating the picker requires opening every DB, and engagement metadata (status, last_opened_at, purpose, export_dir) is routing-level concern that doesn't naturally belong inside per-engagement methodology content. Renaming / soft-deleting / archiving operations on inactive engagements would either require opening each DB or living with a degraded picker. Option C (hybrid: scan the filesystem AND maintain a meta DB cache) was considered briefly and rejected as needlessly complex — the meta DB alone is sufficient.
consequences: v0.5 ships a meta DB at crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements.db (gitignored) with its own Alembic chain. The dogfood migration (DEC-084) creates the meta DB and inserts the CRMBUILDER engagement row. The engagement record lives as a row in the meta DB; the engagement entity schema (DEC-086) follows this placement. The meta DB drift mode is handled by reachability checks; if a per-engagement DB file is missing at activation time, activation fails with a UX message and the user is prompted via the picker. The meta DB's own migrations apply at desktop launch (failure to migrate is a hard-fail UX). Future enhancement (v0.6+, candidate): a small reconciliation tool that detects out-of-band file moves and offers to update the meta DB's engagement records accordingly; not in v0.5 scope.
context: v0.5 Conversation 1's first architectural question (Q1 in the workstream plan §6). v2 needs a mechanism to know what engagements exist on this machine at startup, populate the picker UI, and decide which engagement is active. Two viable options framed: (A) a small bootstrap meta SQLite file at a fixed path with an engagements registry table; (B) directory scan of per-engagement DB files in a known directory. Coupled to the engagement record's location (does it live in the meta DB or inside each per-engagement DB?), to the meaning of operations on inactive engagements, and to drift-recovery posture.
decision: Bootstrap meta DB at the fixed path crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements.db. The meta DB has its own Alembic migration chain (separate from per-engagement chains) and is gitignored. Creating an engagement performs two writes: INSERT into the meta DB's engagements table and create the corresponding per-engagement DB file. Discovery is a SELECT against the meta DB. Engagement endpoints (GET /engagements, POST /engagements, etc.) read and write the meta DB exclusively. The engagement record IS the meta DB row (not a lightweight pointer to records inside each per-engagement DB) — this sub-question is resolved here as well, as a consequence of the meta DB being the canonical home for engagement routing metadata.
rationale: Separates routing metadata ('what engagements exist on this machine') from methodology content ('what's inside an engagement'). Mirrors v1's master-DB pattern (automation/db/master_schema.py), which has held up across two years of v1 evolution. Carries metadata (status, last-opened-at, purpose, export_dir) outside any single engagement's DB so engagement records can be queried, renamed, or archived without opening each per-engagement DB. The 'engagement record inside its own per-engagement DB' alternative is self-referential and complicates the discovery semantics; the meta DB framing is cleaner. The additional SQLite file is a real cost but small; the drift mode (per-engagement DB file moved or deleted out-of-band) is handled by a reachability check at activation time, mirroring v1's automation/core/client_reachability.py pattern.
title: Engagement discovery model: bootstrap meta DB at fixed path with engagements registry table

alternatives_considered: Option B — split (engine DB in engine repo, client DBs in client repos via required engagement_db_path field). Rejected because it requires adding a path field to the engagement record (per Q10) and couples v0.5 to client-repo structure conventions that v0.5's use cases don't actually require. The portability win (client repo cloned to new machine 'comes with' engagement state) is real but theoretical for v0.5's known use cases; deferring it is fine. Option C — convention with nullable override field. Rejected as a compromise that defers a real decision: the field either exists or it doesn't; 'nullable optional override' usually evolves into 'we should have made it explicit one way or the other.' Q2 commits to no field in v0.5; v0.6+ migration adds the field if and when the use case justifies it.
consequences: .gitignore gains crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements/. Engagement record schema (DEC-086) does not include engagement_db_path. The dogfood migration (DEC-084) moves crmbuilder-v2/data/v2.db to crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements/CRMBUILDER.db. When (if) the engagement-DB-travels-with-client-repo use case surfaces, a v0.6+ migration adds nullable engagement_db_path column to the meta DB engagements table; existing rows stay null and continue using convention; new rows or edited rows may set the override. Acknowledged asymmetry with Q8 (exports do split between engine and client repos via a path field) is defensible because DB files are gitignored data while exports are git-tracked documents — different concerns, different homes.
context: v0.5 Conversation 1's Q2. Each engagement's SQLite file needs a location on disk. Three viable options: (A) all per-engagement DB files in the engine repo at a conventional path, (B) split — engine DB in engine repo, client engagement DBs in client repos at {project_folder}/.crmbuilder-v2/{code}.db (mirroring v1's split), (C) convention with optional override field on the engagement record. Coupled to Q8 (exports location) and to whether the engagement record carries a path field. v1 has been doing the split (Option B's equivalent) for two years and it works; v1 originally had a database_path column on Client but deprecated it in favor of convention {project_folder}/.crmbuilder/{code}.db — real-world supporting evidence that convention wins over explicit field at this granularity.
decision: All per-engagement DB files live at crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements/{engagement_code}.db in the engine repo, gitignored. The engagement record carries no path field in v0.5. Dogfood DB after migration is crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements/CRMBUILDER.db; CBM's will be crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements/CBM.db. Filename derived from engagement_code (uppercase per the engagement_code constraint).
rationale: Minimum viable for v0.5; handles both dogfood and CBM cleanly. v1's split is a real precedent worth respecting, but adopting it now adds an engagement-record path field plus a coupling to client-repo structure that v0.5's known use cases (Doug on one laptop, dogfood + CBM active) don't justify. Future migration to v1-style split is small (add nullable engagement_db_path column; null means convention, set means override), so deferring the choice doesn't block it. v1's own deprecation of its database_path column in favor of convention is real-world supporting evidence that the path-field flexibility was not actually used at this granularity.
title: Per-engagement DB file location: engine repo with conventional paths derived from engagement_code

alternatives_considered: Option B — singleton row in the meta DB (a one-row active_engagement table or an is_current boolean column on engagements). Rejected because external clients (curl, MCP tools, diagnostic scripts) cannot peek without an API call or SQL helper, and the file/DB drift risk is low in practice because the desktop is the only writer to both. Option C — environment variable only, no persistence. Rejected because it loses the 'open the app, last engagement is loaded' UX without producing meaningful simplification. The hybrid framing (split the two facts) was the framing change that resolved the conversation; once the two facts were named, the file-for-live + column-for-ordering answer was uncontroversial.
consequences: crmbuilder-v2/data/current_engagement.json added to .gitignore. engagement_last_opened_at column added to the meta DB engagements table (DATETIME, nullable). Activation sequence in multi-engagement-architecture.md §4 includes both writes (file and column). Drift recovery on app launch: if current_engagement.json references an engagement that doesn't exist in the meta DB or whose DB file is missing, the context starts empty and the picker UX prompts for selection (no silent fallback). The in-memory ActiveEngagementContext follows v1's ActiveClientContext pattern (QObject, Qt signal, reachability check on activation, signal emission). External scripts can cat the file to know which engagement is currently active.
context: v0.5 Conversation 1's Q3. v2 needs a mechanism for the desktop, API subprocess, MCP subprocess, and external scripts to know which engagement is currently active, plus a mechanism for the picker to order engagements by recency. Conversation surfaced that two distinct facts are being persisted: live state ('what is the engine pointing at right now', a singleton at the v2-install level, used by API/MCP at startup, by external scripts that want to know who they're talking to, and by the desktop to restore last-active on launch) and ordering ('when was each engagement last opened', per-engagement metadata, used by the picker). Splitting them clarified the framing — the original Q3 framing conflated the two. v1's active-state persistence is itself a hybrid (Client.last_opened_at on master row plus last_selected_client_id in preferences).
decision: Two-channel persistence with each channel canonical for one fact. (1) Live state: a small JSON file at crmbuilder-v2/data/current_engagement.json holds {engagement_identifier, engagement_code, set_at}, atomically written by the desktop on engagement activation. Plus an in-memory ActiveEngagementContext QObject with an active_engagement_changed(object) Qt signal, mirroring v1's ActiveClientContext shape. The desktop reads the file on launch to restore the last-active engagement. (2) Ordering: a last_opened_at DATETIME column on the meta DB engagements table (specifically engagement_last_opened_at per the parent-prefix field-naming convention), updated by the desktop at activation. Used by the picker to surface recent engagements first.
rationale: Mirrors v1's hybrid pattern (Client.last_opened_at on master row plus last_selected_client_id in preferences) which has held up across two years of v1 evolution. The JSON file affords external peek/curl access without an extra API endpoint or SQL helper. The meta-DB column affords queryable ordering for the picker without the API needing to read the JSON file. Separation of concerns matches the underlying reality: ordering is per-engagement metadata; live pointer is a singleton at the v2-install level. The original Q3 framing's three options (file on disk, column in meta DB, env var only) all conflated the two facts; splitting them clarifies that the question is really about the live state, and the meta-DB column is useful regardless.
title: Active-engagement state persistence: JSON file for live state plus engagements.last_opened_at column for ordering plus in-memory ActiveEngagementContext

alternatives_considered: Option B for v0.5 — multi-tenant from the start. Rejected for v0.5 because it requires refactoring every endpoint to accept engagement context (header or path-prefix), refactoring the access-layer connection model, refactoring tests, and reshaping the MCP server — substantial scope addition that v0.5's stated scope did not include. Option D — hot-swap admin endpoint (single API process with POST /admin/switch-engagement). Rejected because it introduces mutable cross-request state that complicates testing and creates mid-request switch race risk; convenience of fast switching isn't worth the testability cost. Option C — dynamic ports per engagement (multiple subprocesses serving simultaneously on different ports). Rejected because v0.5's use case is one engagement active at a time; the port-management complexity isn't justified. For the migration trigger: 'v0.6 specifically' was named but rejected because forcing CBM Phase 1 to wait for the migration is unnecessary scope tightening; 'highest-priority v0.6+ candidate before features' was named but rejected because it under-specifies the trigger; the prototype-to-production framing is what Doug clarified and what fits the v2 charter narrative.
consequences: v0.5 build (Conversation 2 + Claude Code slices) implements the kill-relaunch dance in the desktop's switch path (Slice C in the strawman). The API server itself requires no endpoint refactor for v0.5 — only the addition of meta-DB connection handling for /engagements/* endpoints (per DEC-078, the engagement endpoints serve from the meta DB while other endpoints serve from the active engagement's DB — this two-database API server is documented in multi-engagement-architecture.md §3.10). The MCP server has the same lifecycle. UX cost: a few seconds of API unavailability per switch with a 'Switching engagement...' indicator in the desktop. External clients during the switch see connection-refused or timeout. PI-017 is authored to track the multi-tenant migration with the prototype-to-production trigger. CBM Phase 1 can run on v0.5 (one-process-per-engagement) without waiting for PI-017.
context: v0.5 Conversation 1's Q4 and Q9 (Q9 forced by Q4). The API server today (v0.4) is a subprocess spawned by the desktop at startup, reading CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH from env at startup and serving one DB on port 8765. v0.5 needs to support multiple engagements. Two main viable options: (A) one API subprocess per engagement, bound at spawn, killed and relaunched on engagement switch; (B) multi-tenant single process serving all engagements via header or path-prefix routing. Option D (hot-swap admin endpoint) was named but rejected for mutable-cross-request-state reasons. Q9 (MCP) follows whichever Q4 settles. User directed: approve A for v0.5 but add a decision committing to B. Migration timing was clarified through a brief back-and-forth: not 'v0.6 specifically' or 'highest-priority v0.6+ candidate' but 'when v2 transitions from prototype to production.'
decision: v0.5 ships one API subprocess per engagement (Option A): the API subprocess is spawned by the desktop launcher with CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH set to the active engagement's DB path, serves on port 8765 for that engagement's lifetime, and is killed and relaunched on engagement switch (the desktop's kill-relaunch dance). The API server's existing endpoint code is unchanged from v0.4 — no concept of engagement, no request-routing layer. The MCP server has the same lifecycle (one MCP subprocess per engagement, mirroring the API). Committed for the longer term: multi-tenant single-process server (Option B) is the target architecture. The migration from one-process-per-engagement to multi-tenant is committed; it lands as part of the production-readiness workstream when v2 transitions from prototype to production. This anchors PI-017.
rationale: For v0.5: scope is 'make multiple engagements possible,' not 'redesign the API layer.' Option A delivers the former without doing the latter. The API server code stays as-shipped in v0.4; engagement-routing complexity lives in the desktop's launcher and switcher logic, where new code is going anyway. Consistent with DEC-039's framing ('one v2 instance per engagement, separate SQLite, separate API port'). For the longer term: kill-relaunch is intentional for the v0.x prototype phase but inappropriate for production. Cross-engagement queries, dashboards, mid-flight switching all become desirable as v2 stabilises. Committing to the migration at the prototype-to-production transition avoids the PI-001 anti-pattern (friction triggers that never fire) while keeping the v0.5 scope bounded. The trigger is a planned transition rather than friction signal.
title: v0.5 API and MCP server model: one process per engagement bound at spawn; commitment to multi-tenant migration at prototype-to-production transition

alternatives_considered: Global identifier scope across all engagements (CBM's first session is SES-025+). Rejected as obviously wrong on review/auditability grounds. Engagement identifiers scoped per-engagement (so the dogfood and CBM both have ENG-001). Rejected because engagement records live in the meta DB and there is no per-engagement scope to apply at this level — the meta DB is the single sequence's home.
consequences: No changes to the v0.4 next-identifier helpers themselves; they continue to query the active DB connection and naturally return per-engagement sequences. A new next-identifier helper GET /engagements/next-identifier is added to the v0.5 build, served from the meta DB. CBM's first session record (whenever CBM Phase 1 runs) is SES-001. The dogfood engagement's sessions continue at SES-026+. Identifier collisions across engagements are accepted as not-actually-collisions: they refer to distinct records in distinct engagement scopes.
context: v0.5 Conversation 1's Q5. With per-engagement DBs (DEC-078, DEC-079), the question is whether governance identifiers (SES-NNN, DEC-NNN, PI-NNN, etc.) and methodology identifiers (DOM-NNN, ENT-NNN, PROC-NNN, CRMC-NNN) sequence globally across all engagements or per-engagement. CBM's first session being SES-025 because the dogfood happens to have 24 prior sessions would be absurd; per-engagement scope is the only sane choice for review/auditability. The v0.4 next-identifier helpers (from DEC-068 slice A) query the active DB connection — so per-engagement scope happens automatically without v0.5 helper changes. The remaining question is mostly about confirming the obvious answer.
decision: Governance and methodology identifiers (SES, DEC, PI, RSK, TOP, REF, CHR, STA, DOM, ENT, PROC, CRMC) scope per-engagement: each engagement's DB has its own identifier sequences. CBM's first session is SES-001 within the CBM engagement, independent of the dogfood's SES-025+. Engagement identifiers (ENG-NNN) scope to the meta DB: one sequence per v2 install. On Doug's laptop, the dogfood is ENG-001, CBM is ENG-002, etc. Reinstalling v2 from scratch and recreating engagements resets the ENG sequence; engagement_code values remain the stable human-facing handle.
rationale: Per-engagement scope for governance/methodology is the only sane choice. The v0.4 next-identifier helpers query the active DB connection, so per-engagement scope is automatic with no v0.5 changes to the helpers themselves. Engagement identifiers naturally scope to the meta DB because that is where engagement records live. Identifier collisions across engagements don't matter: engagement A's DEC-001 and engagement B's DEC-001 are distinct decisions in distinct engagements, related only by external context. The meta DB has its own next-identifier helper (GET /engagements/next-identifier) following the v0.4 pattern.
title: Identifier scope: per-engagement for governance and methodology identifiers; engagement identifiers scoped to meta DB

alternatives_considered: Option B — eager at v2 launch. Rejected on correlated-failure-risk grounds and on cost-for-engagements-not-used grounds. Option C — explicit only. Rejected because it creates friction for normal use without a clear safety win; well-tested Alembic migrations applied lazily at activation are the standard pattern.
consequences: v0.5 build adds a helper (call it run_engagement_migrations) that takes an engagement DB path and applies pending migrations. The desktop's activation sequence calls this helper before spawning the API subprocess (Step 3 in multi-engagement-architecture.md §4's 12-step sequence). The activation UX shows an 'Upgrading engagement database...' indicator when migrations are running; for an engagement already at head, the helper is nearly free. Forward-only migrations: same posture as v0.4. The meta DB's own Alembic chain applies at desktop launch — meta DB cannot be opened if its migrations have failed. Acceptance criterion: 'open a stale engagement DB with a newer codebase; activation applies pending migrations; engagement opens with schema at head.'
context: v0.5 Conversation 1's Q6. When a new Alembic migration ships (e.g., v0.6 adds a column to the domain table for domain_short_code per PI-007), each engagement's DB needs the migration applied. Three viable options: (A) lazy at engagement-open — apply pending migrations when the engagement is activated; (B) eager at v2 launch — apply pending migrations to all engagements at desktop startup; (C) explicit only — never automatic, user runs a CLI command. Affects operational UX after every release that ships a migration. v1 uses Option A (run_client_migrations runs at activation per v1's ActiveClientContext).
decision: Lazy at engagement-open. When an engagement is activated, the desktop launcher runs alembic upgrade head against that engagement's DB before spawning the API subprocess. Mirrors v1's run_client_migrations pattern. Each engagement's alembic_version table tracks its own head; no cross-engagement coupling. Engagements that are never opened never get migrated; engagements that are opened after a long pause apply pending migrations at activation. The meta DB has its own Alembic chain that applies at desktop launch (before any engagement is listable) — failure to migrate the meta DB is a hard-fail UX.
rationale: Matches v1's proven pattern. Defers work to when it is actually needed; never-opened engagements pay no cost. Failure modes are scoped to one engagement at a time — a broken migration affects activation of one engagement, not the entire v2 install or all engagements at startup. Eager-at-launch (Option B) creates correlated failure risk (every engagement fails if the migration has a bug, blocking the desktop from launching) and pays cost for engagements the user isn't using. Explicit-only (Option C) creates friction for normal use without a clear win — Alembic migrations applied at known checkpoints (release boundaries) are well-understood, and the engine should 'just work' when the codebase has moved forward.
title: Migrations across engagements: lazy at engagement-open via run_engagement_migrations helper

alternatives_considered: Option B — dual-path compatibility (engine reads from both v2.db and engagements/CRMBUILDER.db forever). Rejected because it accrues permanent technical debt in every code path that opens the dogfood, defeats the Q2 convention, and creates a forever-special-case for the dogfood that the workstream plan explicitly does not want ('the dogfood should be one engagement among potentially several'). Option C — default-engagement special case with engagement_db_path field on the engagement record pointing at the old path. Rejected because it would require reopening Q2's no-path-field decision and would compromise the Q2 convention with a permanent exception just for the dogfood.
consequences: v0.5 build includes a self-contained migration module invoked by the launcher at first-launch detection of the migration-needed state. Slice placement (Slice A foundation versus a dedicated migration slice) is explicitly a Conversation 2 question per the workstream plan §10. Failure recovery: the .pre-v0.5-backup file is the recovery point; the user is told to revert by deleting the new files and reverting code to the prior v2 release. All eight tracked tables plus topics and risks have row counts in the new path matching the source. Idempotency: rerunning after successful migration is a no-op. The dogfood engagement record (CRMBUILDER) is born at migration time with all the field values specified in this decision; engagement.md §3 details the schema and the engagement record values are documented in multi-engagement-architecture.md §3.7 and engagement.md.
context: v0.5 Conversation 1's Q7. The existing crmbuilder-v2/data/v2.db file (which v0.4 ships with) holds the v2-build's own governance data: 24 sessions, 74 decisions, 16 PIs, 66 references, 222 change_log entries, 42 catalog entries, 13 status versions, 2 charter versions, plus topics and risks. v0.5 needs to retrofit this into the new engagement model without losing or corrupting any of it. Three options: (A) one-shot explicit migration with backup-first / verify-after / then-delete discipline; (B) dual-path compatibility (engine knows both old and new paths for the dogfood forever); (C) default-engagement special case (the dogfood's path is allowed to live outside engagements/ via a configurable engagement_db_path field). Coupled to DEC-079 (Q2 chose convention; B and C compromise that retroactively).
decision: One-shot explicit migration runs at v0.5 first launch when the engine detects an existing crmbuilder-v2/data/v2.db and a missing or empty meta DB. The migration is idempotent on rerun. Operations: (1) Backup v2.db to crmbuilder-v2/data/v2.db.pre-v0.5-backup (left in place; user can delete manually after confirming v0.5 works). (2) Create the meta DB at crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements.db and run its initial Alembic migration to head. (3) INSERT the CRMBUILDER engagement row with the v0.5 migration's specified field values (engagement_identifier=ENG-001, engagement_code=CRMBUILDER, engagement_name='CRM Builder v2', engagement_purpose='Dogfood instance hosting the v2 build's own governance content (sessions, decisions, planning items, methodology catalog).', engagement_status=active, engagement_export_dir=absolute path of PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/db-export/ computed from engine repo root, audit timestamps set to migration time). (4) Copy v2.db to crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements/CRMBUILDER.db. (5) Open the new path; query expected row counts for sessions, decisions, planning_items, refs, change_log, charter, status, base_entity_catalog, topics, risks; each count must match the source. (6) Delete the original v2.db (the .pre-v0.5-backup copy remains). (7) Refresh the JSON snapshots at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/db-export/ from the new path. Three install scenarios handled: existing v2 install (migrate), fresh install with no v2.db (no-op; meta DB created empty; first-launch UX guides creating engagements explicitly), rerun after successful migration (detect already-migrated state and exit cleanly).
rationale: Q2 chose convention (DEC-079); Q7 respects Q2's choice rather than carve out a permanent exception. The dogfood deserves the same convention every future engagement gets — both for consistency and because every line of 'is this the dogfood path or the new path?' logic is debt the codebase pays forever. The migration is a one-time cost; the special-case paths in Options B and C are a forever cost. Backup-first / verify-row-counts / then-delete discipline is well-understood standard discipline for destructive migrations, not novel risk.
title: Dogfood migration: one-shot explicit migration moves v2.db to engagements/CRMBUILDER.db at v0.5 first launch

alternatives_considered: Option B — all in engine repo organized by code (db-export/engagements/{code}/). Rejected because reviewers of a CBM engagement's lineage would have to look in two repos (CBM for PRDs and process docs, engine repo for JSON snapshots), defeating the natural-home principle. Option C — manual command only for client engagements (no auto-export for client engagements; dogfood-only auto-export). Rejected because it creates a two-class engagement model where the dogfood is special, contradicting the workstream plan's framing that the dogfood should be one engagement among potentially several.
consequences: engagement_export_dir nullable TEXT column added to the meta DB engagements table. The dogfood migration sets this field for the CRMBUILDER row to the computed absolute path of PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/db-export/. The New-Engagement dialog (built in v0.5 Slice B) includes an engagement_export_dir field (optional; user can leave blank and set later via Edit). v0.4's export hook is rebound to read the active engagement's value. Drift handling: if the path is set but doesn't exist or isn't writable, the export attempt logs a warning and skips; user fixes via Edit Engagement. Null values produce a log warning at activation and no file writes. Absolute paths in the meta DB are fine because the meta DB is gitignored data; machine moves require fresh install and re-creation of engagements, which re-sets paths.
context: v0.5 Conversation 1's Q8. v0.4's db-export JSON snapshots at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/db-export/ are the git-tracked render of the dogfood's content. For v0.5, the question is where each engagement's exports go. Three options: (A) split — dogfood exports stay in the engine repo; client engagement exports land in the client repo via a path field on the engagement record; (B) all in engine repo organized by code (reviewers would have to look in two places for a client engagement's documents); (C) manual command only for client engagements (creates a two-class engagement model). Coupled to Q2 (which chose convention — no path field for DBs) — Q8 accepts an asymmetry because DB files are gitignored data while exports are git-tracked documents.
decision: Exports for each engagement land where the engagement's other documents live. A nullable engagement_export_dir column is added to the meta DB engagements table (TEXT, validated when set: must be an existing writable directory; absolute filesystem path). Dogfood's value is set by the v0.5 dogfood migration (DEC-084) to the absolute path of PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/db-export/ (computed from the engine repo root). Client engagements set their value at creation time via the New-Engagement dialog (user browses to the client repo or types the path). Export-refresh writes to the active engagement's engagement_export_dir; null disables auto-export with a log warning at activation. The existing v0.4 export hook is rebound to read the active engagement's engagement_export_dir.
rationale: Each engagement's git-tracked lineage belongs with the engagement's other documents, where reviewers will naturally look. For the dogfood, that's the engine repo (where the v2-build's PRDs and methodology documents live); for client engagements (CBM, future clients), that's the client repo (where the engagement's process docs, entity PRDs, etc. live). Forcing all exports into a centralised engine-repo location would split each client engagement's documentation between two repos. The asymmetry with Q2 (DB files in engine repo) is defensible because DB files are gitignored data and exports are git-tracked documents — different concerns, different homes.
title: Per-engagement exports: split via nullable engagement_export_dir field on the meta DB engagement record

alternatives_considered: For status lifecycle: Option B (domain pattern candidate/confirmed/deferred with one-way gate). Rejected because engagement records aren't proposed-then-verified; adopting the domain pattern would create awkward UX ('why is my engagement in candidate state? Who verifies it?'). Option C (active/archived, two states). Rejected because it drops the 'temporarily on hold' state which has real operational meaning; users would either keep paused engagements 'active' and remember they're on hold (fragile) or repeatedly archive/unarchive (friction). For the field set: adding engagement_description, engagement_notes, or engagement_db_path was considered for each. Rejected case-by-case as described in the decision text. For the UI shape: a ListDetailPanel in a new 'Engagements' sidebar group was a default candidate; the conversation surfaced that engagement is structurally different and deferred the exact visual layout to Conversation 2 with explicit deviation rationale.
consequences: The meta DB's engagements table has the ten columns specified. The Alembic migration for the meta DB creates this table at v0.5 first launch (or via the dogfood migration's step 2 per DEC-084). v0.5 build (Conversation 2 + Claude Code slices) implements: the access-layer methods (list_engagements, get_engagement, create_engagement, update_engagement, patch_engagement, delete_engagement, restore_engagement, next_engagement_identifier), the eight REST endpoints, the meta-DB connection handling in the API server (the two-database server detail per multi-engagement-architecture.md §3.10), and the engagement UI (switching and management affordances; exact layout from Conversation 2). The dogfood migration sets engagement_status=active for the CRMBUILDER row. New engagements created via the New-Engagement dialog default to active. Free transitions between active/paused/archived are accepted; transitions to values outside the enum return 422 invalid_enum_value. Soft-delete via DELETE sets engagement_deleted_at without touching the per-engagement DB file; restore via POST /restore clears it. Acceptance criteria: 12 testable statements in engagement.md §3.7 covering schema migration, identifier/code constraint enforcement, status enum and transition validation, export_dir validation, access-layer methods, REST endpoints, soft-delete/restore round-trip, meta-DB serving, engagement_last_opened_at updates on activation, and the dogfood/CBM sample-record scenario.
context: v0.5 Conversation 1's Q10 and Q10b. The engagement entity type is the sole new methodology entity in v0.5. Q10 asks for the field set; Q10b asks for the status lifecycle. The kickoff's weak prior for fields (engagement_identifier, engagement_code mirroring v1, engagement_name, engagement_purpose, engagement_status, audit timestamps, possibly engagement_data_dir if Q2 settles on configurable paths) plus three lifecycle options (domain pattern, active/paused/archived, active/archived). Coupled to all prior decisions in this conversation: DEC-078 (engagement record lives in meta DB), DEC-079 (no engagement_db_path), DEC-080 (engagement_last_opened_at column), DEC-082 (ENG identifier scope), DEC-085 (engagement_export_dir column).
decision: The engagement entity type has these properties. Identifier prefix: ENG (3 letters; no collision with existing prefixes; format ENG-NNN zero-padded to 3 digits; server-assigned on POST omission; helper at GET /engagements/next-identifier). Storage: meta DB engagements table at crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements.db. Fields (all prefixed engagement_ per DEC-046): engagement_identifier (TEXT, required, server-assigned, ^ENG-\d{3}$, unique), engagement_code (TEXT, required, regex ^[A-Z][A-Z0-9]{1,9}$ mirroring v1's Client.code exactly, case-insensitive unique within the meta DB), engagement_name (TEXT, required, case-insensitive unique), engagement_purpose (TEXT, required, plain text 1-2 sentences), engagement_status (TEXT, required, default 'active', enum {active, paused, archived}, free transitions), engagement_last_opened_at (DATETIME, nullable, per DEC-080), engagement_export_dir (TEXT, nullable absolute path validated when set, per DEC-085), engagement_created_at / engagement_updated_at / engagement_deleted_at (inherited base behavior). Three deliberate omissions from the domain.md pattern: no engagement_description (purpose suffices), no engagement_notes (engagement is routing metadata not methodology content; scratchpad notes belong inside the per-engagement DB), no engagement_db_path (DEC-079 chose convention). Status lifecycle: active/paused/archived with free transitions, default starter 'active'. Engagement records are direct creations (not propose-verified by a client), so domain's one-way candidate→confirmed gate doesn't fit. Three states capture real operational lifecycle (active work, on hold, wound up); free transitions match reality (engagements can resume from any state). Soft-delete independent of status. No relationships in v0.5. API surface: standard endpoint set (list, get, create, full replace, partial update, soft-delete, restore, next-identifier) served from the meta DB. No activate endpoint — activation is desktop-side orchestration per multi-engagement-architecture.md §4. UI shape: deviates from the spec guide's default Methodology-sidebar-group placement because engagement is structurally different (v2-install-level routing metadata, not domain-scope content); two needed affordances (switching UI and management UI); exact visual layout deferred to Conversation 2 in coordination with the styling workstream (PI-001) per DEC-076's boundary discipline. Full spec at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/methodology-schema-specs/engagement.md with 12 acceptance criteria in §3.7.
rationale: Field set follows DEC-046 (parent-prefix naming) and the established methodology-entity-schema-design conventions, adapted for engagement's structural difference. engagement_code mirroring v1's Client.code exactly (^[A-Z][A-Z0-9]{1,9}$, length 2-10) lets v1 and v2 co-reference the same engagement by code if useful later. The three omissions (description, notes, db_path) are justified by engagement's nature as routing metadata rather than methodology content. Lifecycle: engagement records are direct creations not propose-verified, so domain's one-way gate doesn't fit (Option B). Three states capture the real operational lifecycle without ceremony; two states (Option C) drops 'paused' which has real-world meaning. Free transitions match reality (an engagement on hold can resume; an archived engagement can be reactivated if a client comes back). UI deviation justified by engagement's structural difference from the four v0.4 methodology entities; the Methodology sidebar group is for domain-scope content, not for v2-install-level routing metadata, and engagement needs two distinct affordances (always-visible switcher and occasional-access manager) rather than one master/detail panel.
title: Engagement entity schema, identifier, status lifecycle, and API surface

alternatives_considered: Compact density (~20-22px rows, 13px body) — rejected because at 4K HiDPI compact reads undersized rather than dense, and Doug's workstation has no screen-real-estate scarcity to optimize for. Comfortable density (~30-34px rows, 14-16px body) — rejected because it wastes pixels at 4K and doesn't match a power-user-tool user model.
consequences: Every spacing token in styling-design-pass.md §1.1 derives from Default density. The 4px base unit and 9-step spacing scale (0/4/8/12/16/20/24/32/40/48) inform every padding, margin, and gap decision downstream. Button geometry (6×14px padding, 88px minimum width) ensures Save/Cancel buttons line up in dialog rows. The Inter Variable body size is 14px (which holds up at Default density; would have needed 13px at compact, would have allowed 16px at comfortable). Implementer reading the design pass can produce QSS without re-deriving any spacing value.
context: The styling Conversation 1 design pass opened against the kickoff prompt with density as the most-upstream token-level decision — it shapes every spacing token in the system. The kickoff's weak prior framed density as a real fork worth pausing on. Three concrete options were on the table: compact (~20-22px list rows, 13px body text, IDE-aesthetic — VS Code / JetBrains feel, recovers screen real estate at the cost of needing a body font that holds up at small sizes), default (~26-28px rows, 14px body, Qt-native-aligned, comfortable for daily use), comfortable (~30-34px rows, 14-16px body, consumer-SaaS feel, wastes screen on power users). v2 today renders with Qt-default density and no deliberate spacing rhythm; the design pass needed to settle this before any other token-layer work.
decision: **Default density.** Concrete derived values: list row height 28px (master panes — QTableView, QTreeView, QListView); form field height 28px (text inputs, combos); button padding 6×14px with 88px minimum width; panel chrome 16px outer padding; inter-pane (master ↔ detail) spacing 12px; dialog margins 20px outer + 12px between rows + 16px above buttons; body text 14px. Spacing scale: 4px base unit with scale 4 / 8 / 12 / 16 / 20 / 24 / 32 / 40 / 48. See styling-design-pass.md §1.1 for the full token specification.
rationale: The initial Claude recommendation was compact (power-user tool, single primary user, list-dense panels where dense rows recover daily scan time). The recommendation was overridden by Doug's workstation context: 3× 4K monitors at a workstation, rarely uses a laptop. At 4K HiDPI, compact density would feel undersized rather than crisp (HiDPI scaling already tightens visual rhythm relative to logical pixels), and there is no scarcity of screen pixels to optimize for. Default sits in the middle, native to Qt's own assumptions, and renders crisply across all three monitors. Comfortable was the other extreme and was rejected on the same no-scarcity logic — wasting screen on a power-user tool is the wrong direction.
title: Density target: Default — workstation context rules out compact and comfortable

alternatives_considered: Keep navy #1F3864 (Option A) — rejected as default-by-accident; ratifying it would skip the question of whether it's actually right. Brighter Tailwind-like blue #2563EB — rejected as consumer-SaaS-marketing leaning; doesn't match the deliberate-but-restrained design direction. Non-blue accent (Option C — green / purple / plum) — rejected for visual continuity reasons; v0.4 screenshots use navy, and a non-blue accent would feel like a wholly different app. Grayscale-only (Option D) — rejected as too restrained for a tool with frequent selection and status indication needs; chroma is required for selection signals and status colors regardless. Warm or neutral gray family — rejected for less-cohesive pairing with the cool blue accent.
consequences: color.accent.default retires the legacy #1F3864 navy in two existing places: the sidebar's _STALE_DOT_COLOR in sidebar.py and the _BANNER_STYLE in dialogs/error.py. The four ad-hoc grays in panels/*.py (sessions, risks, topics, decisions, planning_items) and base/crud_dialog.py map to the new neutral scale during the styling build: #f4f4f4 → color.neutral.100, #666 → color.neutral.700, #888 → color.neutral.500, #444 → color.neutral.700. The destructive #c1272d (and disabled #b6868a) on reference-delete and crud-dialog delete buttons retire to color.danger.default + the disabled-gray treatment from §2.5. The _WARNING_STYLE = 'color: #B22222;' on panels/processes.py retires to color.warning.default (amber, not the legacy red — the soft-deleted-domain message is informational warning, not error). The retirement is mechanical search-and-replace in the build phase plus the token-aware widget refactors. WCAG AA contrast verified for every text-on-background combination in styling-design-pass.md §4.4.
context: v2 at v0.4 ship has exactly one deliberately-chosen chroma color: the navy #1F3864 stub, used in two visible-to-user places — the sidebar stale-indicator dot (via QPainter on a QPixmap) and the error-dialog banner title text. Navy was never deliberately chosen as a brand accent; it was the closest at-hand 'make this feel official' pick during v0.1/v0.2 build. The styling Conversation 1 needed to settle (a) whether to ratify navy or pick a deliberate accent, (b) which color family to commit to, (c) whether to harmonize neutrals with the accent. Plus a 9-step neutral gray scale to replace the four ad-hoc grays currently in code (#f4f4f4 for read-only field backgrounds, #444 for read-only text and one of the dialog text variants, #666 for the dialog read-only text variant, #888 for secondary labels and captions).
decision: **Cool-blue family.** Specifically color.accent.default = #1F5FBF (a moderately-saturated, slightly-lighter blue than the legacy navy stub) with derived variants: hover #2A6CCE, pressed #184F9F, subtle #E8F0FB (used for tinted-background treatment on selected sidebar/list rows), focusring color.accent.default at 40% alpha (used for keyboard-focus outline rings). Paired with a 9-step cool-gray neutral scale: color.neutral.0 #FFFFFF (modal backgrounds, contrast surfaces), neutral.50 #F7F9FB (primary panel surface — the dominant 'page' color), neutral.100 #EEF1F5 (subtle surface — alternating rows, sidebar background, read-only field background — retires the legacy #f4f4f4), neutral.200 #DDE3EA (hairline borders, table cell dividers), neutral.300 #C1CAD4 (disabled control fill, placeholder text), neutral.500 #7A8694 (secondary text and captions — retires the legacy #888), neutral.700 #3F4854 (read-only field text — retires the legacy #444 and #666), neutral.800 #272D36 (default body text), neutral.900 #0F1318 (high-emphasis text, headings). Drafted status colors harmonized with the cool blue: color.danger.default #C0392B (destructive button fill — retires legacy #c1272d), color.danger.text #A8281C (inline error/warning text — retires legacy #B22222), color.danger.subtle #FBEBE8, color.warning.default #B0731A (amber), color.warning.subtle #FBF2E0, color.success.default #2D7A4D (rare in v2).
rationale: Four options weighed: keep #1F3864 navy (ratifies a default-by-accident color), pick a different blue (preserves family identity but signals deliberate choice), pick a non-blue accent (deep green / warm purple / muted plum — more distinctive but breaks visual continuity with existing v0.4 screenshots), grayscale-only with no chroma accent (too restrained for clear selection signals in a tool with frequent selection states). Different blue won. Follow-up sub-pick between brighter Tailwind-like #2563EB and steelier #1F5FBF: chose the steelier value on the reasoning that it matches a 'serious tool, deliberate choice' read better than the Tailwind-bright value, which leans consumer-SaaS-marketing-site. Cool grays follow naturally for pairing with a cool blue accent — warm grays would create slight visual tension, neutral grays read coherent but less unified. The 9-step scale was drafted in conversation as a set of values mapping to the recurring uses in v2's existing code, with deliberate retirement of the four ad-hoc gray values that had accumulated organically. Status colors were drafted to harmonize with the cool blue accent (slightly less orange-shifted than typical alert reds; amber rather than pure orange; muted green) and replace the legacy red and warning treatments in dialog and panel code.
title: Brand accent #1F5FBF (steely blue) plus 9-step cool-gray neutral family

alternatives_considered: Flat tokens (`color.accent.default = '#1F5FBF'`) — pure YAGNI. Rejected because the cost of the alternative structure today is negligible (~5 LOC) and the retrofit cost if a theme variant ships is non-trivial (rename every color token in code, refactor every consumer to be theme-aware).
consequences: The tokens module (forthcoming in the build phase, expected at crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/ui/tokens.py) exposes a theme-keyed dict. A small accessor function or method serves as the consumer interface. Adding a future dark-mode theme requires authoring a `'dark'` dict with parallel values and exposing a theme-toggle mechanism — but does not require renaming any tokens or refactoring any consumer code. Future palette variants (CBM-branded, print-friendly, etc.) follow the same additive-only pattern.
context: Color tokens can be structured as flat values (`color.accent.default = '#1F5FBF'`) or as theme-keyed dicts (`TOKENS['light']['color.accent.default'] = '#1F5FBF'`). The workstream plan §3.2 puts dark mode out of scope as an implementation, deferred to a separate PI if anyone asks for it. The styling Conversation 1 kickoff flagged dark-mode anticipation at the token-naming layer as a real fork. The remaining question to settle was the structural choice: whether to pay a small one-time cost to structure tokens such that adding dark mode later requires only adding values, or to keep tokens flat and accept the retrofit cost if dark mode is ever needed.
decision: **Theme-keyed dict structure.** Tokens live in a dict keyed by theme name; the single theme today is `'light'`. Consumers access tokens through a small accessor function (`tokens.get('color.accent.default')` or similar) that reads `TOKENS[current_theme][key]` internally. Adding dark mode would be adding a `'dark'` sibling dict with parallel values; consumers don't change. No dark-mode values are authored in styling-design-pass.md today. The structure does not preclude them.
rationale: We're building the tokens module from scratch in the build phase. Adding the theme-key dict layer costs one extra dict key today and one accessor function (~5 LOC). The alternative cost — flat tokens today, then retrofit if dark mode ever ships — is renaming every color token in code plus refactoring every consumer to be theme-aware. The structural choice is essentially free insurance against the future PI. The theme-keyed structure also pays an immediate non-dark-mode dividend: it cleanly separates 'what the values are' (data) from 'how they're consumed' (interface), which makes future palette experiments trivially additive — a CBM-branded palette variant, a print-friendly variant, etc., become 'add a theme dict' rather than 'fork and rename the consumer code.' This decision was handled as decide-and-announce rather than full eight-element pause because the cost differential today is negligible and the outcome differential only matters in worlds where dark mode (or a palette variant) eventually ships.
title: Theme-keyed token-naming structure, light theme only today but dark-mode-ready without retrofit

alternatives_considered: System-default fonts (status quo) — rejected for cross-platform divergence. Other open sans serifs considered briefly: Geist (Vercel's, OFL — 'Vercel deploy panel' aesthetic), IBM Plex Sans (IBM's, OFL — 'IBM enterprise' aesthetic), Source Sans 3 (Adobe's, OFL — 'Adobe creative tool' aesthetic), JetBrains Sans (newly open-sourced — 'IDE-ish' aesthetic). Each carries different aesthetic baggage; Inter was preferred as both ubiquitous and aesthetically neutral. Distinctive/mannered fonts considered briefly: Berkeley Mono (monospace-as-default — IDE/terminal aesthetic), Public Sans (US Web Design System — 'civic tech'), Atkinson Hyperlegible (Braille Institute — low-vision-optimized). Rejected as too mannered for v2's intended user-tool aesthetic. Alternative monospaces (Fira Code, IBM Plex Mono, Cascadia Code) were adequate but JetBrains Mono pairs aesthetically with the chosen design direction.
consequences: Two font binaries committed to the repo at crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/ui/assets/fonts/ (or equivalent path settled in the build phase): Inter-Variable.ttf and JetBrainsMono-Variable.ttf. Two `QFontDatabase.addApplicationFont()` calls in the app startup path. `QApplication.setFont(QFont('Inter'))` sets the default. Per-widget overrides for monospace contexts via `setFont(QFont('JetBrains Mono'))` on `QLineEdit`s for identifier columns and on the About dialog's path-value labels. Cross-platform README screenshot consistency. Size, weight, and line-height scales are drafted in the design pass; refineable at build time without re-opening Conversation 1.
context: v2 today renders with `QApplication.font()` default, which resolves to whatever the host OS provides — SF Pro Text on macOS Sonoma+, Segoe UI Variable on Windows 11, Ubuntu Sans / Liberation Sans on Linux. The mismatch between platforms makes README screenshots feel 'off' when viewed on a different platform, and design tokens for line-height and letter-spacing tuned for one font don't quite hit on the others. The styling Conversation 1 needed to settle font family choice at both the sans-serif (primary UI font) and monospace (identifier columns, code surfaces, About dialog paths) layers.
decision: **Inter Variable as the sans-serif throughout the application; JetBrains Mono Variable as the monospace.** Both bundled in the repo as binary font files and loaded at app startup via `QFontDatabase.addApplicationFont()`. Inter set as the default via `QApplication.setFont(QFont('Inter'))`. Both fonts are OFL-licensed (free, redistributable). Total binary cost ~530 KB (~280 KB Inter + ~250 KB JetBrains Mono). Drafted size scale (font.size tokens): caption 12px (audit timestamps, sub-secondary text) / small 13px (master-pane row text, secondary form-field labels) / body 14px (default body, form fields, dialog text) / body_large 16px (detail-pane emphasized values) / heading_3 18px (detail-pane section headers, dialog section labels) / heading_2 22px (dialog title bars) / heading_1 28px (reserved). Drafted weight scale on Inter Variable's weight axis: regular 400 / medium 500 / semibold 600 / bold 700. Drafted line-height scale: tight 1.2 (headings) / normal 1.45 (body) / relaxed 1.6 (long-form prose — rare in v2). See styling-design-pass.md §1.3.
rationale: Inter is the modern-dev-tool default for cross-platform consistency — used by Linear, Figma, Stripe internal tools, GitHub Primer, shadcn/ui, and effectively the convention for any new web/desktop product targeting a 'designed' aesthetic. Bundling costs are small: ~280 KB binary, one font-load call at app startup. Inter's variable-font format provides the full weight axis (400 through 700) without bundling separate font files per weight. The retrofit cost if v2 ships with system-default fonts and switches to bundled later is also low (a one-line change in app startup), but consistency in README screenshots and overall coherence improves immediately with bundling. JetBrains Mono adds at the monospace layer for identifier columns (DEC-077 and similar align cleanly by their numeric suffix) and for the About dialog's path values where the current code already implicitly assumes a monospace font but doesn't enforce one.
title: Font stack: Inter Variable + JetBrains Mono Variable, both bundled at app startup

alternatives_considered: Fully flat throughout (Option A) — no shadows, no overlay backdrop. Rejected because flat-aesthetic modals can fail to register on Linux compositors with minimal window decoration. Broader elevation tiers (Option C) — modals + popup combos + selected list rows + focused buttons, multiple z-tiers. Rejected for platform-rendering inconsistency and the discipline cost of maintaining multiple elevation profiles.
consequences: Every modal dialog's `__init__` adds a `QGraphicsDropShadowEffect` setup pattern. The backdrop overlay is a new small widget that paints over the main window content while a modal is active. The radius.dialog token (6px, drafted in §1.4) pairs with the soft shadow on platforms that render rounded dialog corners. No QSS box-shadow rules anywhere — shadows come exclusively from the QGraphicsDropShadowEffect API. The build phase will need to verify that the shadow renders acceptably on macOS, Ubuntu (Yaru / Gnome / KDE), and that the backdrop fade-in feels smooth. Implementation complexity: low; a small helper function `apply_dialog_elevation(dialog)` plus the backdrop widget.
context: Today modals open directly over the underlying panel with no visual separation other than the OS-drawn window frame. The dialog sits in the same z-plane visually; the eye has to use the title bar and window outline to register 'this is a modal.' Flat-aesthetic v2 risks modal-disambiguation failure on some Linux compositors where window decorations vary. The styling Conversation 1 needed to settle whether v2 has any visual depth at all and, if so, where it applies. QSS box-shadow doesn't render reliably in PySide6, so any drop-shadow approach uses either Qt's built-in QGraphicsDropShadowEffect (simple, performs adequately, applies to any QWidget) or custom paintEvent overrides (heavyweight, platform-fragile). Workstream plan §3.2 explicitly puts custom paintEvent out of scope; QGraphicsDropShadowEffect remains within the QSS-supplemented-by-built-in-effects posture.
decision: **Elevation only on modal dialogs.** Every `QDialog` subclass — CRUD dialogs, references attach/delete, version-replace, About, error dialog — gets a `QGraphicsDropShadowEffect` applied in `__init__`. Single elevation token `shadow.dialog` applied uniformly: Y-offset 4px, X-offset 0px, blur radius 16px, color color.neutral.900 at 25% alpha. Plus `overlay.modal_backdrop` — a full-window overlay widget at color.neutral.900 8% alpha that fades in over ~150ms when a modal opens, dimming the underlying panel. Rest of the app — sidebar, panels, master pane, detail pane, fields, buttons — shares one z-plane and stays fully flat. No popup-shadow tier, no focused-button micro-elevation, no selected-row shadow.
rationale: Modal disambiguation is the single highest-value depth cue in an otherwise flat application. One Qt effect setup per dialog (3-5 lines) plus one design token plus one backdrop widget resolves the most common flat-design failure mode (modal-vs-underlying-panel confusion, especially on Linux compositors with variable window decorations) without committing to multi-tier elevation discipline anywhere else. Going broader — popup shadows on combo dropdowns, focused-button micro-elevation, selected-row shadows — would buy aesthetic polish at the cost of platform-rendering inconsistency. Popup shadows in particular render inconsistently across Qt platforms and clip oddly under some Linux compositors. The single-tier approach is the right level of investment for v0.5/v0.6 timeframe.
title: Modal elevation only — single shadow.dialog token via QGraphicsDropShadowEffect; rest of app flat

alternatives_considered: Qt-built-in / system-theme icons via QIcon::fromTheme (Option A) — rejected for cross-platform inconsistency (works on Linux, empty on macOS/Windows). Phosphor (Option B) — rejected for aesthetic mismatch with the restrained design direction (warmer rounded weight reads 'modern product' rather than 'deliberate tool').
consequences: Initial set of ~10-15 SVGs committed to the repo. Small icon-loading helper in ui/icons.py (or similar) exposes a `lucide(name, size=16, color_token=...)` interface returning `QIcon`. Per-icon color application happens at runtime via the design token system — the same SVG asset renders in color.neutral.700 for a default button and color.neutral.0 for a primary button. Icon set grows additively as components need new icons during the build phase; no separate process needed to request additions. Build phase verifies the QSvgRenderer-to-QIcon conversion handles HiDPI correctly across the three target platforms.
context: v2 today is effectively icon-free — context menus render text-only (Edit / Delete / Restore / Go to references / Copy identifier — all words, no icons), toolbars render text-only, dialog buttons render text-only, sidebar entries are text-only. As the styling pass settles component visual decisions (context menus, button affordances for 'Add reference,' status-indicator dots, possibly sidebar group icons in future), v2 needs a consistent icon source. Three options were considered: Qt-built-in / system-theme icons via `QIcon::fromTheme` (free, available, but inconsistent on Linux and largely empty on macOS/Windows without explicit fallback paths), Phosphor (~7,000 icons across 6 weight variants — thin/light/regular/bold/fill/duotone — MIT-licensed, warmer rounded aesthetic), Lucide (~1,400 icons, single regular stroke at 2px uniform width, ISC-licensed, minimal/restrained aesthetic, used by shadcn / Vercel / many modern dev tools).
decision: **Lucide.** Icons bundled additively in the repo at crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/ui/assets/icons/lucide/{kebab-case-name}.svg as components need them — no speculative pre-bundle of all 1,400 icons. Small loader helper wraps `QSvgRenderer` to produce `QIcon` at requested size, with runtime color tinting via the design token system. Default icon size 16px (matches body text height closely; readable at 4K HiDPI). Initial wave of ~10-15 icons anticipated to cover the surfaces designed in Conversation 1: pencil (Edit), trash-2 (Delete), rotate-ccw (Restore), external-link (Go to references), copy (Copy identifier), plus (Add / New), x (Close / Dismiss), chevron-right / chevron-down (collapsible toggles, sort indicators), circle-alert (warning), circle-x (error), check (success affirmation, checkbox checked state), asterisk (required-field marker). Sketch of the loader interface: `def lucide(name: str, *, size: int = 16, color_token: str = 'color.neutral.700') -> QIcon: ...`.
rationale: Lucide's minimal 2px-uniform-stroke aesthetic pairs cleanly with Inter and the restrained color direction (cool blue accent, cool grays, deliberate-but-quiet design language). Phosphor's warmer rounded weight reads slightly mismatched with this direction — Phosphor's aesthetic is 'modern designed product,' Lucide's is 'deliberate restrained tool,' and Lucide matches v2's positioning better. Qt-built-in icons via QIcon::fromTheme work inconsistently on Linux (depend on the installed icon theme — Yaru / Adwaita / Breeze all differ) and return empty on macOS/Windows without explicit fallback paths — effectively text-only-on-most-platforms in practice. Lucide's single-weight constraint means fewer choices to discipline during build (no per-context weight selection); every icon renders at the same visual weight. Total binary cost is small — Lucide icons are tiny (~1-2 KB each) and the initial bundle is ~10-15 icons (~20 KB total).
title: Lucide icon library, bundled additively as components need icons

alternatives_considered: Tinted background only (Option C in sidebar conversation) — rejected because hover risks blurring with selected unless hover is designed to be meaningfully different. Full-fill with accent (Option B) — rejected as heavy in long lists. Right-edge accent bar (Option D, GitHub-style) — rejected as less conventional than left-bar; would mismatch user expectations from VS Code / JetBrains / Linear / shadcn. Border outline (Option E) — rejected as reading more like a button than a selected row. Different selection vocabularies for sidebar versus master pane — rejected; system coherence wins over per-surface optimization. For master pane banding: alternating row shading (Option B) — rejected as appropriate for high-density spreadsheet contexts, overweight for v2's 22-row-visible panes. Pure minimal with no dividers (Option A) — rejected as feeling under-organized for a data-table surface.
consequences: A shared `QStyledItemDelegate` subclass (forthcoming in the build phase) renders the left-bar + tint pattern across all 12 panels' master views (eventually 13 once v0.5 adds the engagement panel). The sidebar's QListWidget either inherits a similar delegate or gets paint-event overrides. Both implementations are called out in styling-design-pass.md §3.1 as high-attention surfaces for the build phase. The soft-deleted-row visual (when 'include deleted' is on) renders text in color.neutral.500 with a leading Lucide trash-2 icon at 14px in the identifier column; this also belongs in the shared delegate. Identifier columns across all master panes render in font.family.mono (JetBrains Mono) at font.size.small so identifiers like DEC-077 align cleanly by their numeric suffix. The keyboard-focused-state outline drawing on master-pane rows is constrained inside the cell area, not crossing the row divider — a subtle build-phase implementation detail.
context: The sidebar and the master pane (the per-panel `QTableView` / `QTreeView` showing records) are the two surfaces in v2 where users see 'selected' most often. Today both rely on OS-default selection treatment — system blue rectangle on macOS Sonoma+, orange-tinted on Ubuntu Yaru, etc. — inconsistent across platforms and undifferentiated from each other. The styling Conversation 1 needed to settle the visual vocabulary for selected state in both surfaces. Coupled decision because using different vocabularies in the two places would force users to learn two patterns; using the same vocabulary ties the design system together. Several visual options were on the table: tinted background only (restrained but risks blurring with hover), full-fill with accent color and white text (heavy in a long list), left-edge accent bar plus tinted background (modern dev-tool convention — VS Code, JetBrains, Linear, shadcn), right-edge accent bar (GitHub-left-nav style), border outline (reads more like a button). For the master pane specifically, additional sub-question of whether to use alternating row shading (zebra striping) for visual structure.
decision: **Left accent bar plus tinted background, applied to both sidebar entries and master-pane rows.** 3px-wide vertical bar in color.accent.default (#1F5FBF) flush against the row/entry's left edge, plus color.accent.subtle (#E8F0FB) tinted background, plus text bump to color.neutral.900 at font.weight.medium for slight emphasis. The master pane requires a custom QStyledItemDelegate subclass to render the left bar (Qt's default item rendering does not support this pattern through QSS); the sidebar similarly needs a delegate or paint-event override on QListWidget. Master pane specifics: row dividers (1px hairline color.neutral.200 below each row) but no alternating row shading; column header background color.neutral.100 with bottom hairline color.neutral.200 and font.weight.semibold color.neutral.700 text. Sidebar specifics: distinct hover treatment (color.neutral.200 background, no left bar) so hover and selected never blur. Keyboard-focused state on both surfaces reuses the selected visuals plus a 1px color.accent.focusring outline drawn outside the bounds. See styling-design-pass.md §§2.1 and 2.3.
rationale: The left accent bar plus tinted background is the modern dev-tool convention for good reason: it gives selection a strong, peripheral-vision-readable signal without dominating the visual field. Full-fill with accent (alternative B in conversation) reads heavy in a 12+ item sidebar — the eye repeatedly snaps to whichever single item is selected. Tinted-background-only (alternative C) risks blurring with hover unless hover is meaningfully different (which then requires designing two separate treatments without a clear hierarchy). Border outline reads more like a button than a selected row. Right-edge bar is GitHub-style but less ubiquitous; left-bar is the broadly-recognized convention. Reusing the same vocabulary across sidebar and master pane keeps the design system tight: users learn 'selected = left bar + tint' once, and the rule applies wherever they encounter it. For the master pane's banding sub-question, middle posture (row dividers, no alternating shading) was chosen over banded (which is appropriate for high-density spreadsheet-like data — v2's master panes show ~22 rows at default density on 4K, low enough that the eye does not lose its place) and over fully minimal with no dividers (which would feel under-organized for a structured data table).
title: Selected-state visual vocabulary: 3px left accent bar plus tinted background; consistent across sidebar and master pane

alternatives_considered: Apply design system with no special treatment (Option A) — rejected as under-leveraging the natural identity moment. Full showcase with custom brand mark (Option C) — rejected because brand-mark authoring is a separate design problem unsuitable for remote conversation resolution, and v2 has no established mark to evolve; introducing a brand mark mid-styling-pass would derail the pass without producing a deliberately-considered mark. Modest showcase wins as the threading-the-needle option.
consequences: The existing QFormLayout in about_dialog.py is restructured to a QVBoxLayout with a header block (wordmark + tagline labels) followed by a vertical list of metadata rows followed by the close button row. The header block is a small custom widget combining the two labels with specific font tokens. Metadata rendering shifts from form-style (label-left-of-value) to two-line list-style (label above value). Path/URL values use JetBrains Mono via the monospace font token. About dialog version-reading logic (importlib.metadata with __version__ fallback per CLAUDE.md v2 version-source convention) is unchanged. The styling Conversation 2 may revisit the tagline wording at build time without re-opening Conversation 1; the design pass commits the wordmark + tagline structure but explicitly notes the tagline as refineable. About dialog becomes a natural early build slice in §3.3's suggested order.
context: The styling Conversation 1 kickoff named the About dialog as a 'small showcase' surface — the smallest, most-contained dialog, well-suited to demonstrating the design system end-to-end. Today the About dialog (about_dialog.py) is a pure `QFormLayout` with bold labels (Application / Version / API base URL / Database path / Snapshot directory) and selectable value labels, rendered with Qt defaults — no design intent. Min width 440px. The styling pass needed to settle how much to elevate the About dialog visually. Three options: apply design system tokens but no special treatment (under-leverages the natural identity moment), modest showcase with wordmark + tagline header but no logo (visible identity without brand-mark authoring), full showcase with custom brand mark in addition to wordmark + tagline (requires authoring a logo).
decision: **Modest showcase.** Standard dialog chrome (per §2.7) plus a header block at the top of the dialog body containing the application wordmark 'CRMBuilder v2' in font.size.heading_2 (22px), font.weight.semibold, color.neutral.900, followed by a tagline below the wordmark (`space.1` of vertical separation) in font.size.small (13px), color.neutral.500, regular weight. Proposed tagline (refineable at build time): 'Declarative CRM deployment and methodology authoring.' Alternatives noted in §2.8 as 'CRM deployment and methodology tooling for consultants' and 'End-to-end CRM implementation tooling.' Metadata table restructured from the existing QFormLayout to a vertical two-line-per-row list: line 1 is the label in font.size.small font.weight.medium color.neutral.500 (sentence-cased, not bold-capitalized); line 2 is the value in font.size.body color.neutral.800 (paths and URLs in font.family.mono / JetBrains Mono at font.size.small color.neutral.700). Values remain selectable so users can copy paths — preserves current behavior. Close button at the bottom-right of the dialog body, single secondary button per §2.5. Minimum width preserved at 440px.
rationale: The About dialog is the natural identity moment in the application — what someone sees when they ask 'what is this app?' Treating it like every other dialog (Option A — no showcase) feels under-leveraged. But authoring a brand mark (Option C — full showcase with logo) is a separate design problem with its own subjective tradeoffs: logos are notoriously hard to settle by remote conversation, and v2 has no established mark to evolve. Option B threads the needle: the wordmark plus tagline gives the dialog visible identity without requiring brand-mark authoring, and the wordmark is already the application's name set in plain Inter Semibold (no additional design subtask needed). The shift from QFormLayout to a two-line-per-row vertical list also exercises the design system end-to-end (label-above pattern from §2.4, monospace token usage from §1.3, neutral text colors from §1.2.3) on the smallest contained surface — making the About dialog a natural early build slice.
title: About dialog modest-showcase treatment: wordmark plus tagline header, retiring the QFormLayout default

alternatives_considered: Option 1: four slices per the workstream-plan strawman (foundation+migration in one, engagement entity panel in another, switching, closeout). Rejected because the 'engagement entity panel' in the strawman bundled both the REST API and the panel UI, producing an oversize prompt mixing access-layer/API/validation work with UI/CRUD-dialog work. Option 2: six slices per kickoff §5.3's granular framing. Rejected because separating the dogfood migration into its own slice creates a non-functional intermediate state at slice A's completion (the foundation infrastructure exists but the dogfood is still at the old path; running v0.5 in that state requires a fallback path that adds code complexity for a state that exists for only one slice). Option 3: eight slices splitting the activation worker, the picker, and the single-gesture flow into separate slices. Rejected because the tight coupling among these components would require multiple fake-coupling-point slices.
context: v0.5 Conversation 2's slicing question (Q9 in the kickoff). Workstream-plan strawman from §5 had four slices (foundation+migration combined, engagement entity panel, switching, closeout). Alternative granular framing from kickoff §5.3 had six slices (config foundation, dogfood migration standalone, schema+access+REST, panel UI, switching+mechanism, closeout). Slice count and content directly affect build-time cadence: too tight makes each prompt oversize and increases the cost of any one slice's failure mode; too granular increases prompt-and-merge overhead and creates non-functional intermediate states. The question's two-part test applied: real downstream impact (build cadence and acceptance-gate granularity over the next several days of execution) AND two viable options producing meaningfully different outcomes (four vs six vs five-with-different-cuts). Decided via the consequential-decision template.
