decision: Five slices: A — foundation infrastructure (meta DB, ActiveEngagementContext, two-database API wiring, lazy-migration helper, sidebar Engagements group container, refresh-service extension) PLUS dogfood migration combined in one slice; B — engagement schema (dataclass, status enum), access layer (eight standard repository methods with validation), and REST API (eight standard endpoints with envelope shape, JSON snapshot hook); C — engagement management panel UI (ListDetailPanel subclass, master/detail panes, CRUD dialogs, forbid-active-delete with last-engagement edge case, refresh-service registration); D — switching mechanism (top-strip widget, picker dropdown, 12-step ActivationWorker with question-6 amendment, activation overlay) plus single-gesture creation+activation flow (NewEngagementDialog extending slice C's EngagementCreateDialog with file creation and activation steps); E — closeout (version 0.5.0 bump, README release note, end-to-end integration smoke test, final regression pass, manual smoke verification). Slice A monitored for size at execution time; A1 (foundation only) / A2 (dogfood migration) split is the documented fallback if the slice A prompt exceeds healthy size.
rationale: Five slices balance build cadence with acceptance-gate granularity. Combining foundation with dogfood migration in slice A is justified because foundation alone is a non-functional intermediate state (the meta DB exists but no engagement records and dogfood data is still at the old path; the desktop cannot operate against this state without falling back to the old single-DB path or failing); separating them would require either a non-functional intermediate slice or a hidden fallback path in slice A's code, both of which are worse than combining. Splitting slice B (schema+API) from slice C (panel UI) is justified because each is substantial on its own — B's eight REST endpoints with full validation logic plus the JSON snapshot hook is a complete subsystem; C's master/detail panel plus three CRUD dialogs plus the forbid-active-delete edge cases is a complete UI surface — and combining them produces an oversize prompt with mixed concerns. Keeping the switching mechanism (slice D) as one slice rather than splitting picker from activation worker is justified because the picker and activation worker are tightly coupled: the picker click triggers the activation worker, the activation overlay binds to worker progress signals, and the single-gesture flow uses the activation worker as its third step; splitting would require defining a fake activation API in slice C/D and a backfill in the next slice. The A1/A2 split fallback is documented but not pre-committed; the decision is made at slice A's prompt-drafting time if size signals warrant.
title: v0.5 slice breakdown: five slices with foundation+migration combined

alternatives_considered: Option B: sidebar-only with everything in the Engagements group. Rejected because switching becomes a multi-click sequence (sidebar → engagement panel → row → 'Activate' action) when it should be a single-click affordance. Option C: modal-only switching invoked from the sidebar with no top-strip. Rejected because modal-only switching breaks the user's mental model — switching is a transient action, not a configuration dialog, and forcing a modal interrupt for every switch is friction without payoff. Option D: top-strip-only without a sidebar group. Rejected because management UI discoverability suffers; new users wouldn't know where to find the panel. Option E: place the Engagements group below the existing sidebar groups instead of above. Rejected because v2-install-level scope is structurally above per-engagement scope; placement below would suggest the opposite hierarchy.
context: v0.5 Conversation 2's two UI-placement questions (Q1 and Q2 in the kickoff), settled together because the affordances are coupled. Q1: where does the switching affordance live? Q2: where does the management affordance live? The engagement entity is structurally different from the four v0.4 methodology entity types (v2-install-level routing metadata vs domain-scope methodology content), so it doesn't fit the Methodology sidebar group introduced in v0.4. Switching is a frequent action requiring always-visible affordance; management is a less-frequent action requiring discoverable affordance. The question's two-part test applied: real downstream impact (every user interaction with v0.5 touches one or both affordances) AND two viable options producing meaningfully different outcomes (top-strip vs sidebar-only vs modal). Decided via the consequential-decision template.
decision: Three coordinated affordances: (1) always-visible top-strip switching widget positioned above the sidebar entries inside the sidebar container; renders the active engagement's name with code in parentheses and a Lucide chevron-down caret; clicking opens the picker dropdown. (2) Picker dropdown anchored below the top-strip; live engagements ordered by engagement_last_opened_at descending; paused and archived rendered in color.neutral.500 and sorted to bottom within their bucket; active engagement marked with a Lucide check icon; footer item 'Manage engagements...' separated by hairline divider, opens the management panel. Empty state ('No engagement selected' top-strip; picker contains only the footer item) handled. (3) Engagements sidebar group positioned above Governance with one entry titled 'Engagements' that opens the management panel — same panel that the picker footer opens. The management panel is a ListDetailPanel subclass per the v0.4 pattern: master pane with five columns (Identifier, Code, Name, Status, Last Opened); detail pane with form fields per engagement.md §3.6.3; standard CRUD operations including the forbid-active-delete behavior per DEC-101.
rationale: The top-strip placement gives switching an always-visible affordance distinct from the sidebar's content-navigation function. Switching is potentially frequent (multiple times per work session if a consultant is working across multiple engagements); requiring a sidebar click would add friction. The Engagements sidebar group entry is the discoverability path for users who treat the sidebar as their primary navigation; without the entry, the management UI would be reachable only through the picker's footer item which is less discoverable. Dual paths to the management panel (sidebar entry + picker footer) reduce friction without creating navigational ambiguity — both paths arrive at the same panel. The sidebar group's position above Governance reflects the v2-install-level scope distinction (engagement records are routing metadata across all engagements, structurally above the per-engagement governance and methodology content). Picker ordering rules (live first by last-opened-at descending; non-live at bottom; active marked) match user expectations for an MRU-style picker. The empty-state handling is required for the fresh-install case where the picker has nothing to select from.
title: Engagement UI affordance placement: top-strip switching widget plus Engagements sidebar group plus dual paths to management panel

alternatives_considered: Option B: two-gesture flow with explicit Create then Activate affordances. Rejected because every creator would have to choose 'now or later' twice (Create dialog and then a separate Activate action), which is friction without payoff for the dominant use case (create-then-immediately-work-in). Option C: single-gesture with all-or-nothing rollback (any failure rolls back to no-engagement-created state). Rejected because activation failure after both creates succeed is a realistic transient state (port-release timeout, MCP launch failure, etc.) where rolling back the engagement record forces re-data-entry; preserving the record with a retry-or-stay choice is better UX. Option D: single-gesture with implicit retry on activation failure. Rejected because hidden retry behavior produces opaque latency and obscures real failures from the user; explicit retry is better.
context: v0.5 Conversation 2's engagement-creation flow question (Q3 in the kickoff). Creating a new engagement requires three operations: POST /engagements (creates meta DB row), desktop creates per-engagement DB file with Alembic at head, desktop initiates 12-step activation. The question is whether to expose these as a single user gesture (one click triggers all three) or as separate gestures (user clicks 'Create', then later clicks 'Activate'). The question's two-part test applied: real downstream impact (creation is a recurring action; the choice shapes every engagement-creation user experience) AND two viable options producing meaningfully different outcomes (single-gesture vs two-gesture). Decided via the consequential-decision template.
decision: Single-gesture creation+activation: the New Engagement dialog runs all three operations behind one user click on Submit. Three-label progress indicator advances through 'Creating engagement record...' → 'Initializing database...' → 'Switching to <name>...' Each label transitions to a Lucide check icon on success or a Lucide circle-x in color.danger.default on failure. Validation errors at each stage roll back appropriately: POST failure stays in the dialog with inline error per the v0.3 validation-error pattern; file-creation failure DELETEs the meta DB row to roll back; activation failure after both creates succeed surfaces inline with 'Try switching now' and 'Stay in <previous engagement>' affordances and the engagement record persists. The slice-C EngagementCreateDialog (which creates the meta DB row only) remains in the codebase as a parent class for NewEngagementDialog; the slice-D NewEngagementDialog is the primary path for engagement creation after slice D lands, but EngagementCreateDialog is still available for scripted use cases (e.g., creating a record without activation for testing or recovery scenarios).
rationale: Single-gesture creation matches the user's mental model: creating an engagement implies wanting to work in it; forcing a separate 'now activate it' gesture adds friction without payoff. The three-label progress indicator gives a coherent narrative for the few-second sequence; the labels advance fast enough to read as confirmation of progress rather than as a slow operation. The graceful failure-recovery affordances handle the one realistic partial-success state (engagement record and file exist but activation failed) cleanly without silent rollback; the user explicitly chooses whether to retry or to stay in the previous engagement. The slice-C dialog remaining in the codebase as a parent class enables the slice-D extension via inheritance rather than duplication, and supports edge-case use through direct invocation.
title: Single-gesture engagement creation+activation flow with graceful inline failure recovery

alternatives_considered: Option B: accept the operation and auto-switch to another engagement before completing the soft-delete. Rejected because the auto-switch target is ambiguous (most-recent? alphabetical? what if there's only one other? what if it's soft-deleted?); making the user choose via the picker is cleaner. Option C: hard error with no redirect (just refuse). Rejected because the message would leave the user wondering what to do next; the redirect affordance is small additional cost for substantial UX gain.
context: v0.5 Conversation 2's active-engagement-soft-delete question (Q4 in the kickoff). When the user attempts to soft-delete the engagement that is currently active, two postures are possible: (a) refuse the operation and redirect to switching first, or (b) accept the operation and auto-switch to another engagement before completing the soft-delete. The question's two-part test applied: real downstream impact is minor (the operation is uncommon by use-case design) AND only one option is genuinely defensible (refusing is cleaner; auto-switch introduces choosing-which-engagement-to-switch-to as a hidden decision). Decided as decide-and-announce.
decision: The engagement panel's Delete dialog detects whether the target row is the currently-active engagement (via comparison against ActiveEngagementContext.engagement_identifier). When active, the standard edge-text confirmation field is replaced with an inline message: '<engagement_name> is currently active. Switch to a different engagement first, then soft-delete this one.' The Delete button is replaced with a 'Switch engagement' button that opens the picker dropdown. Last-engagement edge case (the active engagement is the only one on the install): message changes to '<engagement_name> is the only engagement on this install. Create another engagement before soft-deleting this one.' The button changes to 'Create engagement' opening the New Engagement dialog (single-gesture variant in slice D, slice-C Create dialog before slice D lands). The next-launch drift-recovery path in engagement.md §3.4.5 remains as a safety net for cross-restart desync (e.g., user soft-deletes the active engagement via direct API call or external script, then restarts the desktop; current_engagement.json points at a soft-deleted engagement; in-memory context starts empty; picker prompts for selection).
rationale: Refusing the operation is cleaner than auto-switch: the user explicitly chooses which engagement to switch to (via the picker) rather than the system silently picking 'next most recent' or similar. Inline redirect (replacing the Delete button with 'Switch engagement') keeps the user in the same UI context, avoiding modal-stacking. The last-engagement edge case wording handles the corner case where switching isn't possible, redirecting to creation. The drift-recovery safety net handles out-of-band deletion that bypasses the dialog. The operation is uncommon by use-case design (consultants don't typically delete the engagement they're working in), so the additional friction of refuse-and-redirect is acceptable.
title: Forbid soft-delete on the currently-active engagement with inline redirect to switch first

alternatives_considered: Option B: pre-fill with a computed path like ~/Documents/crmbuilder/{engagement_code}/db-export/. Rejected because the path would be wrong for the dominant use case (client-repo-based) and right for an edge case (consultant-local-only). Option C: pre-fill with an absolute path computed from a configuration setting. Rejected because v0.5 has no such configuration setting and adding one for a default isn't justified.
context: v0.5 Conversation 2's export-dir-default question (Q5 in the kickoff). The engagement_export_dir field on the engagement record is nullable per engagement.md §3.2; when set, it points at an absolute filesystem path where the engagement's JSON snapshots are auto-written. The New Engagement dialog needs to handle the default state: pre-fill with a computed path (e.g., a subdirectory of the engagement-code-named home directory)? Leave blank? The question's two-part test applied: real downstream impact is minor (this is a configuration default, not a behavior change) AND only one option fits the Q8-of-Conversation-1 framing where null disables auto-export. Decided as decide-and-announce.
decision: The New Engagement dialog's export-dir field is empty by default with placeholder text 'Optional — leave blank to disable auto-export.' and tooltip 'Where this engagement's JSON snapshots will be written. Recommend a path inside the client repo so exports travel with the engagement documents.' Null is a first-class value (disables auto-export; the access-layer validates this case in slice B and the activation-time export check in slice D logs a warning if null is the value). The dogfood migration in slice A explicitly sets a non-null value for CRMBUILDER (the engine-repo db-export/ path computed at migration time); the migration's behavior is divergent from the dialog default because the dogfood engagement has a definite and computable export location while user-created engagements typically need explicit configuration of where exports should land.
rationale: Null default avoids guessing what the user wants (any pre-filled path would either need to be a sensible default for most use cases or would mislead users into accepting a value they hadn't actually chosen). The placeholder text makes the optionality explicit. The tooltip provides discoverability for the recommended pattern (path inside the client repo) without forcing it. The CRMBUILDER divergence from the dialog default is justified because the dogfood has a known export location (engine-repo db-export/) that the migration knows about; user-created engagements don't have an analogous always-correct default.
title: Null default for engagement_export_dir on new engagement records

alternatives_considered: Option B: no JSON export for the meta DB; engagement panel refreshes via explicit API polling or via signals fired by access-layer writes only. Rejected because it breaks uniformity with the v0.3+ pattern and creates a special-case refresh mechanism for one panel. Option C: bundle the meta DB export into the dogfood's db-export/ as a single engagements.json at the parent level. Rejected because the dogfood's db-export/ is scoped to CRMBUILDER's content; mixing in install-level metadata produces a confusing artifact and a coupling between the dogfood and the meta DB that doesn't otherwise exist. Option D: place the meta DB export outside db-export/ entirely. Rejected because doing so departs from the established v0.3+ JSON-snapshot pattern without justification.
context: v0.5 Conversation 2's meta DB export location question (Q10 in the kickoff, tightly coupled to Q7's engagement panel refresh trigger). The meta DB hosts the engagements registry table; v0.3's JSON-snapshot pattern (DEC-022, DEC-008) makes writes regenerate snapshot files under db-export/ that panels file-watch for refresh. The question is whether the meta DB participates in this pattern and where its snapshot lands. The question's two-part test applied: real downstream impact is moderate (the snapshot location affects how the engagement panel refreshes and where the artifact lives in the repo) AND there are two viable options producing meaningfully different outcomes (bundled into the dogfood's db-export/ vs separate location). Decided as an operational refinement.
decision: Meta DB has its own JSON export at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/db-export/meta/engagements.json. The db-export/meta/ subdirectory lives parallel to the dogfood's engagement-content exports at db-export/ (which are sessions.json, decisions.json, planning_items.json, etc. — the CRMBUILDER engagement's content). Writes to the meta DB regenerate engagements.json via the standard access-layer hook synchronously within the same transaction. The path is fixed at the engine-repo level (not configurable per engagement, because the meta DB is install-scope routing metadata, not engagement-scope content). The engagement panel registers with the refresh service and refreshes on file change per the standard v0.3+ pattern.
rationale: Uniformity with the v0.3+ JSON-snapshot pattern: the meta DB participates in the same render-on-write discipline as per-engagement DBs. The meta/ subdirectory makes the scope distinction visible (install-level routing metadata vs engagement content) — a future reader of the db-export/ directory tree can distinguish 'this is CRMBUILDER's content' from 'this is install-level engagement registry' without reading the file contents. Bundling the meta DB export into the dogfood's db-export/engagements.json is conceptually wrong because the dogfood's per-engagement DB doesn't contain engagement records; engagement records live in the meta DB; mixing them produces a confusing artifact. Placing the meta DB export outside db-export/ entirely (e.g., at crmbuilder-v2/data/exports/) would require a separate file-watch pattern outside the v0.3+ convention.
title: Meta DB JSON exports at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/db-export/meta/engagements.json

alternatives_considered: Alternative scope shapes were not explicitly evaluated as alternatives — the scope was iteratively refined during the conversation through the ten settled questions and the consequential-decision presentations. The PRD shape (13 sections vs the kickoff's suggested 8-section shape) was chosen to match v0.4's shape per the kickoff's 'Shape follows ui-PRD-v0.4.md' direction.
context: v0.5 Conversation 2's PRD approval gate (Q12 implicit in the kickoff's deliverables list). The PRD at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/ui-PRD-v0.5.md and the companion implementation plan at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/ui-v0.5-implementation-plan.md and the five slice build prompts at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-v2-ui-v0.5-{A..E}-*.md were drafted in this conversation and presented for review. The PRD opens 'Draft — pending approval'; this decision records the transition to Approved.
decision: v0.5 UI PRD v0.5 approved: 10 in-scope items (multi-engagement routing infrastructure; dogfood migration; engagement entity type with eight standard endpoints; 12-step activation sequence with question-6 amendment; Engagements sidebar group and management panel; switching affordance with top-strip and picker; single-gesture engagement creation; forbid-active-delete with last-engagement edge case; meta DB JSON exports with file-watch refresh; About-dialog version bump and README release note), 16 out-of-scope items (multi-tenant API+MCP per PI-017; engagement-level access control; v1 Client import; cross-engagement queries; optional engagement_db_path override; engagement rename; hard delete with file removal; additional engagement-level fields; User Process Guide v0.2; auto-switch on active-engagement soft-delete; right-click 'Activate' on engagement panel; full styling design pass execution; methodology entity types beyond v0.4's four; methodology entity renderers; catalog FK integration; v1 NOT_SUPPORTED reimplementation workstreams), five-slice plan (A foundation+migration combined; B engagement schema+access+REST; C panel UI; D switching+single-gesture; E closeout), 10 acceptance criteria for slice E closeout plus the 13 architecture-level and 12 entity-level acceptance criteria distributed across slices A-D. Implementation plan companion approved at the same time. Five slice build prompts approved at the same time. PRD §9 coordination section rewritten at closeout to reflect the styling Conversation 2 v0.6 PRD that landed mid-conversation: v0.5 ships first per DEC-095's separate-release sequencing; v0.6 retrofits engagement panel and all other surfaces as the 13th panel covered by v0.6 slice C. PRD transitions from Draft to Approved.
rationale: The PRD and implementation plan and five slice prompts together cover everything needed for v0.5 execution: requirements (PRD), build sequencing (implementation plan), step-by-step execution guidance (slice prompts). The release scope matches the workstream-plan and Conversation 1's architectural foundation; the slice plan optimizes for build cadence while preserving non-trivial acceptance gates; the deferred items have justified rationales (cited PIs, future versions, scope discipline). The PRD's 11 risks and mitigations and 5 open questions are appropriate for a build-time-tunable release. The §9 rewrite captures the actual coordination posture with v0.6 (which crystallized mid-conversation when the styling Conversation 2 deliverables landed on origin/main) rather than the pre-clarification posture that the original draft assumed.
title: v0.5 UI PRD approval — release scope and slice plan accepted

alternatives_considered: **Option A — Bundle into v0.5.** Slice prompts go under CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-v2-ui-v0.5-{X}-*.md and interleave with engagement-management slices (estimated v0.5 slices: A-D engagement management + E-I styling = nine slices). One v0.5 release ships when BOTH workstreams complete — the slower one gates the release. One README section. One closeout. Rejected on navigability grounds: bundling permanently blurs the release-version index that v2 uses for git log, README, decisions navigation, and MCP status reporting. The marginal savings (one fewer closeout cycle, ~half day) didn't compensate for the permanent blurring cost.
consequences: Slice prompts named under CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-v2-ui-v0.6-{A..F}-*.md. PRD at ui-PRD-v0.6.md; implementation plan at ui-v0.6-implementation-plan.md. __version__ in crmbuilder-v2/src/crmbuilder_v2/__init__.py bumped to '0.6.0' at slice F closeout (slice F handles the bump). Two README release-note sections — v0.5 entry plus v0.6 entry. Two closeouts — v0.5 closes out independently when its slices land; v0.6 closes out via slice F + the post-slice-F operator steps (status versioned-replace, SES-030 application). Engagement-panel coupling: v0.5 ships first; v0.5's engagement panel ships with Qt-default styling; v0.6 slice C picks up the engagement panel alongside the other 12 existing panels in the omnibus retrofit slice. Build-planning decisions taken in this conversation (DEC-106, DEC-107) are recorded at this same close-out (SES-030).
context: The styling workstream's build planning needed to settle a question the workstream plan §6 named explicitly: whether the styling work ships bundled into v0.5 (one release containing engagement management + visual design system) or as a separate v0.6 release that lands shortly after v0.5. Both releases were in flight at the time of this conversation — v0.5's engagement management was in architectural planning, the styling design pass had just closed (SES-027), and Conversation 2 (this conversation) was opening to plan the styling build. The decision had to be made before slice file naming, PRD file naming, or implementation plan file naming could be settled — those depend on whether the styling work is v0.5 slices E-I or v0.6 slices A-F. The workstream plan author's weak prior leaned toward separate v0.6.
decision: **Ship as a separate v0.6 release.** v0.5 ships engagement management alone (slices A-D, four slices) and closes out independently when engagement work completes; __version__ bumps to '0.5.0' and v0.5 closes via its own session-record + apply prompt. Styling builds in parallel under CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-v2-ui-v0.6-{X}-*.md with its own A-F six-slice ordering. When styling completes, __version__ bumps to '0.6.0' and v0.6 closes out. Two README sections, two closeouts. Engagement-panel coupling resolved per workstream plan §4 'v0.5 ships first' branch — slice C's panel retrofit work picks up the engagement panel alongside the other 12 existing panels. Follow-on detail committed in the same decision turn: styling release PRD at ui-PRD-v0.6.md; implementation plan at ui-v0.6-implementation-plan.md; slice prompts under prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-v2-ui-v0.6-{A..F}-*.md; __version__ after v0.6 closeout '0.6.0'; release order v0.5 first then v0.6; v0.5 engagement panel inherits whatever design tokens have shipped at slice-land time; design-pass decisions (DEC-087..DEC-094) stand as-is.
rationale: Release versions are v2's primary navigation index — git log, README, decisions table, __version__, MCP-exposed status all index by version. To date v2 has held one clean functional theme per release: v0.1 storage system, v0.2 base CRUD, v0.3 references + sessions write surface, v0.4 polish + methodology panels. Bundling makes v0.5 the first v2 release with two unrelated themes welded together, which permanently blurs the project's navigability — anyone (including Doug in a year) asking 'what changed in v0.5' gets a two-track answer where the simple cases have been giving one-track answers. Methodology rule at stake: the workstream plan §6 already named 'release timing' and 'closeout coordination' as the considerations, and the author's weak prior leaned toward separate. The design pass document didn't pick either way. The bundling cost (two unrelated themes under one version) is permanent in the project's history; the separate-release cost (one additional closeout cycle, roughly half a day of administrative work) is real but bounded. Functional independence of the two workstreams is load-bearing.
title: Version bundling for the styling work: ship as separate v0.6 release, not bundled into v0.5

alternatives_considered: **Option A — Stay close to the workstream plan five-slice strawman (Foundation / Governance retrofit / Methodology retrofit / Dialog polish / Closeout).** Rejected on three grounds: master-pane delegate buried in panel-retrofit slices creates artificial governance/methodology coupling; redundant review work in two parallel retrofit slices doing the same pattern; status and crash banner work muddled into dialog-polish slice. **Option B — Use the design pass §3.3 eight-step ordering more directly (Foundation, Sidebar, Master pane delegate, Governance retrofit, Methodology retrofit, Dialog polish, Status/warning + crash banner, Closeout).** Rejected because the design pass author already acknowledged the ordering as approximate; collapsing it to six slices reduces closeout overhead without losing material structure. **Option C — Hybrid with reserved override mechanism.** Rejected on YAGNI grounds at decision time (later partially rewalked back: the Topics-panel tree-view delegate override in slice B used a class-attribute override pattern, justified pragmatically because Topics is a concrete v0.6 caller, not speculative).
consequences: Slice prompts authored at PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/prompts/CLAUDE-CODE-PROMPT-v2-ui-v0.6-{A..F}-*.md, lengths 456 / 369 / 398 / 495 / 369 / 304 lines respectively. Per-slice acceptance criteria specified in ui-PRD-v0.6.md §6 (A1-A7, B1-B7, C1-C7, D1-D6, E1-E6, F1-F7) plus cumulative roll-up. The strictly sequential dependency chain means slice F cannot start until slice E lands; slice E cannot start until slice D lands; etc. The engagement panel coupling between v0.5 and v0.6 is resolved in slice C: by the time slice C runs, v0.5 has shipped per DEC-105 and v0.5's engagement panel is part of the omnibus retrofit. Per-slice screenshot capture protocol per DEC-107 applied across slices A-E; slice F adds the automated WCAG contrast verification.
context: After DEC-105 settled the version-bundling question (separate v0.6), the styling build needed a slice structure. The workstream plan §5.3 had sketched a five-slice strawman (Foundation / Governance retrofit / Methodology retrofit / Dialog polish / Closeout). The design-pass document §3.3 had a more granular eight-step suggested order. These needed reconciliation, but the design pass already conceded 'approximate; Conversation 2 may restructure slice boundaries based on file-conflict topology with v0.5 work running in parallel.' The decision was about how to package the work — same surfaces, same tokens, same components either way, but different review-burden distribution and different file-conflict risk profiles.
decision: **Six slices A-F.** Slice A: Foundation + About dialog (token module, font and icon asset bundling, loader helpers, modal elevation infrastructure, base widget hooks, About dialog re-skin as the canary surface). Slice B: Sidebar + master-pane delegate (the shared selected-state vocabulary per DEC-093, applied via custom QStyledItemDelegate on the sidebar QListWidget and via a shared QStyledItemDelegate subclass registered on every panel's master view). Slice C: Panel retrofits + ReferencesSection (omnibus retrofit covering all 12 existing panels plus v0.5's engagement panel; label-above form layout EVERYWHERE via QFormLayout.WrapAllRows; ReferencesSection sub-sectioned plain-list rewrite). Slice D: Dialogs + form controls (five button categories with full state coverage via dynamic buttonCategory property; internal context strip on record-editing dialogs; combo dropdown popup + checkbox custom indicator; delete-confirm body treatment). Slice E: Status, error, warning + crash banner (WarningCallout widget; Processes panel retoken from danger-red to warning-amber; error dialog header retoken; crash banner folded into the design system). Slice F: Closeout (version bump, README, WCAG contrast test, regression pass, integration smoke). Strictly sequential dependency chain A→B→C→D→E→F; no parallelism within v0.6 because each slice depends on tokens, hooks, or categories established by prior slices.
rationale: Three reconciliation differences from the workstream plan §5.3 strawman: (1) the master-pane delegate is pulled to its own slice B rather than buried in panel-retrofit slices — the delegate is shared across all 12+ panels; putting it in panel-retrofit slices would artificially couple governance and methodology retrofit work (one of them would land the delegate and the other would inherit it); pulling it forward to its own slice lets slice C be a single omnibus retrofit instead of two; (2) governance and methodology retrofits collapsed into one slice C — the retrofit pattern is identical across all 12 panels; splitting it repeats the same review work twice without buying anything once slice B has landed the shared delegate; (3) status surfaces + crash banner promoted to their own slice E — the workstream plan would have folded these into 'dialog polish' (slice D), but the crash banner currently lives outside the design system and the inline-error treatment cuts across many panels; bundling with dialogs muddles two different concerns. The sequencing also accounts for the engagement panel coupling: v0.5 ships first per DEC-105, so by slice C's land time the engagement panel exists and is picked up by the omnibus retrofit alongside the other 12 panels.
title: Slice structure for v0.6: six slices A-F reconciling the workstream-plan strawman

alternatives_considered: **Automated pixel-measurement or QSS-introspection checks.** Rejected as overkill at v0.6's scale — building snapshot test infrastructure or QSS-property assertions adds significant code without buying meaningful regression catch beyond what eyeball + screenshots already provide. **Eyeball-only without screenshots.** Rejected on history-loss grounds — if no screenshots are committed, retrospective work has no concrete reference for 'what changed visually in slice X' beyond the design pass document and the code diff. **Screenshots gitignored.** Rejected: defeats the retrospective purpose. **Multiple WCAG runs (per-slice rather than closeout-only).** Rejected because tokens don't change between slices (read-only after slice A per PRD §8); one run at slice F is sufficient.
consequences: PRD §6 acceptance criteria for each visual slice (A1-A7, B1-B7, C1-C7, D1-D6, E1-E6) include the screenshot-commit requirement; PRD §6 slice F includes the WCAG check requirement (F4). Implementation plan §7 specifies the six-step screenshot capture protocol Doug follows after running each slice's Claude Code prompt. Each slice's commit message template instructs Claude Code to 'notify Doug for screenshot capture' as the final step. Slice F's prompt-runner picks the specific WCAG library (wcag-contrast-ratio recommended) and authors tests/crmbuilder_v2/ui/test_token_contrast.py parameterized over the design pass §4.4 combinations. If the warning-on-white combination fails AA at body size (design pass §4.4 flagged it as borderline at caption size), the recommended remediation is a token-value adjustment from #B0731A to #9D6517 — closes PRD Open Question #4.
context: v0.6 is visual work with different acceptance signals than functional work. For v0.4 and prior, 'slice done' meant tests pass and new functionality works. For visual surfaces, the test-pass signal is weaker (visual rendering is hard to assert in unit tests without expensive snapshot infrastructure), and eyeball verification by Doug is more meaningful. The styling Conversation 2 needed to settle the per-slice and per-release acceptance pattern.
decision: **Per-slice after-state screenshots plus eyeball verification, plus a single automated WCAG AA contrast check at slice F closeout.** Each visual slice (A through E) commits after-state screenshots of each affected surface to PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/styling-screenshots/slice-{X}/. Screenshots captured by Doug after running the slice's Claude Code prompt; committed in a separate operator commit immediately after the prompt's commit lands. Filename convention {surface-name}.png (e.g., about-dialog.png, sidebar.png, master-pane-table.png, master-pane-tree.png, master-pane-soft-deleted.png, sessions-panel.png, etc.). Total expected count across v0.6: ~15-20 PNGs at ~100-300 KB each, ~3-5 MB committed to git. Slice F adds an automated WCAG AA contrast verification test at tests/crmbuilder_v2/ui/test_token_contrast.py using the wcag-contrast-ratio Python library (with hand-rolled fallback if the library is unavailable), parameterized over the text-on-background combinations from design pass §4.4 (A9). The WCAG check is a build gate per this decision — failures are not tolerated; if any combination fails, a token-value adjustment lands in ui/styling.py and slice F re-runs.
rationale: For a solo developer with the right aesthetic eye and Doug's three-4K-monitor workstation context, eyeball verification is the appropriate primary signal for visual work — automated pixel-measurement or QSS-introspection checks would be overkill at this scale. Screenshots become project history and release-note material: a year from now, asking 'what did the sidebar look like at the end of slice B?' has a concrete answer in styling-screenshots/slice-B/sidebar.png. Per-slice (rather than only release-final) screenshots also support retrospective work — if a v0.7+ regression is suspected, the slice-by-slice progression is visible in git history. The single automated check (WCAG contrast) is the one accessibility commitment v0.6 makes per design pass A9; running it once at slice F closeout is sufficient because token values are read-only after slice A merges (per PRD §8 constraint). The git-tracked storage cost (~3-5 MB) is the intended trade for project navigability.
title: Slice acceptance pattern for v0.6 visual work: per-slice screenshots + closeout WCAG check

alternatives_considered: **Option (a) — Auto-pick when unambiguous.** API enumerates engagements via the meta DB; if exactly one exists, route to it, persist to current_engagement.json, log prominently, start. If zero or two-plus, fail loud. Rejected on no-silent-fallback grounds. **Option (c) — Hybrid first-time-only.** Auto-pick once on the very first install when no marker exists AND exactly one engagement exists, then persist; from then on require explicit marker; if deleted later, fail loud rather than re-derive. Rejected as too clever — operators can't tell from current filesystem state whether the next API start will auto-pick or fail without knowing marker history.
consequences: Fresh-install workflow gains one step: open the UI once or pass --engagement once to seed the marker before the standalone shell-launch path works. Slight ops-doc burden; doesn't affect normal operation since the UI handles marker creation automatically on engagement activation. The slice A prompt implements the fail-loud path in cli.py:run_api(); the error message text references --engagement <code> (DEC-111) which lands in the same slice, so the message is honest as written. Doc note: the desktop UI's Engagements panel writing current_engagement.json on activation is the canonical seed mechanism for fresh installs.
context: The slice D multi-tenancy work in v0.5 introduced per-engagement DBs at crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements/{engagement_code}.db and a marker file at crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements/current_engagement.json that the desktop UI writes to record the active engagement. The CLI entry point crmbuilder-v2-api (cli.py:run_api) never reads the marker — it reads Settings defaults plus any CRMBUILDER_V2_* env vars present in the shell. Post-v0.5-migration, the legacy v2.db has been deleted and SQLAlchemy+SQLite auto-create an empty file on connection; the result is the API silently lands on an empty file and every request 500s on missing tables (Investigation Bug 1, surfaced during the SES-001 paper-test apply attempt on 05-19-26). The fix extracts a resolve_active_engagement() helper that reads the marker; the question is what to do when the marker is absent (fresh install, marker deleted, marker points at a missing code).
decision: **Fail loud always.** The API refuses to start without a valid current_engagement.json. Operator must open the desktop UI's Engagements panel to activate an engagement (which writes the marker as a side effect), or pass --engagement <code> as a CLI flag (DEC-111). Exit code 2 (configuration error). Error message: 'No active engagement. Activate one via the desktop UI's Engagements panel, or pass --engagement <code> when running the API standalone.' If the marker exists but points at a code not in the meta DB, the message names the missing code: 'Active engagement \'X\' not found in meta DB. Activate a valid engagement via the desktop UI or pass --engagement <code>.' Errors are written to stderr via print(..., file=sys.stderr) and also to stdout for UI subprocess capture, before uvicorn binds the port.
rationale: The bug being fixed is silent fallback to v2.db; the fix should not introduce a new silent fallback under a different name. Auto-picking when exactly one engagement exists (Option a) is silent at exactly the moment loudness is wanted — if a second engagement is later added in a way that bypasses the UI (e.g., a script-created restore, a migration that runs in the background), auto-pick suddenly routes to whichever engagement happens to be first in enumeration order. The hybrid first-time-only auto-pick (Option c) creates state operators cannot reason about from filesystem inspection — they can't tell whether the next start will auto-pick or fail without knowing the marker's history. The system already has a clean mechanism for declaring active engagement (the marker file) and the desktop UI writes it correctly; the question is whether to require operators to use that mechanism or paper over its absence in narrow cases. The kickoff was explicit that this passes the two-part test for consequential decisions; the eight-element template applied.
title: Fresh-install behavior when no active engagement marker exists: fail loud always

alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — Per-engagement scratch dir fallback.** Route helper computes crmbuilder-v2/data/engagements/{code}/db-export/ as a default when engagement_export_dir is null and sets CRMBUILDER_V2_EXPORT_DIR to that path. Writes succeed; snapshots land in the scratch dir. Logged prominently at route-time. Rejected because silent fallback to a scratch dir is silent fallback by a different name; the warning-band affordance in slice B is the right UX for forcing intent rather than auto-inventing a path.
consequences: New engagements created without engagement_export_dir cannot be written to until the operator configures one (probably one Edit Engagement click for a new engagement). The UI's Open Engagement flow needs to surface this clearly — yellow warning band per slice B's affordance scope — so operators see why their commit failed before they hit the failure. Test fixtures that create engagements need to either supply an export_dir or expect the new exception. The gate uses the sentinel '__UNCONFIGURED__' as the env-var value when null in meta DB; about_dialog renders this as '(not configured)' for human consumption (slice B affordance). Empty string in the meta DB treated as null throughout.
context: Investigation Bug 2 surfaced that the export hook writes snapshots against Settings.export_dir which resolves engagement-blindly to the engine-repo default. When CBM was the active engagement during the SES-001 paper-test apply attempt, the CBM-content snapshot wrote to PRDs/product/crmbuilder-v2/db-export/ (CRMBUILDER's path) instead of CBM's configured engagement_export_dir. CBM.db retained the records correctly; only the snapshot side was misrouted. The Settings-route fix (DEC-110) makes Settings.export_dir resolve per-engagement, but the question of what to do when the active engagement has engagement_export_dir = null in the meta DB is a separate behavior decision. The schema allows null because operators may create an engagement without immediately committing to a snapshot target.
decision: **Refuse the write.** When route_settings_to_engagement(code) finds the active engagement's engagement_export_dir is null/empty in the meta DB, the helper sets CRMBUILDER_V2_EXPORT_DIR to a literal sentinel value '__UNCONFIGURED__'. The new gate helper assert_export_dir_ready(s) checks for this sentinel at every active export-write site (session_scope, force_export, catalog exporter — see DEC-113) and raises EngagementExportDirNotConfigured if present. The exception message: 'Engagement \'{code}\' has no engagement_export_dir configured. Set engagement_export_dir for the active engagement via the desktop UI's Edit Engagement dialog, or activate a different engagement.' Reads (GET endpoints, the UI sidebar, the panels) work normally; only the export-write path is gated. The UI's Open Engagement flow surfaces a yellow warning band at activation time so the operator sees it before their first write.
rationale: Same class of reasoning as DEC-108. The bug being fixed is silent fallback to the wrong path; the safest replacement for the bug is not 'silent fallback to a different path' (the option-b scratch-dir approach), it's loudness. The option-b argument (silent fallback to a per-engagement scratch dir prevents cross-engagement clobbering) is real but it solves a different problem: it prevents Bug 2's specific cross-engagement clobbering, while leaving in place the silence that masks misconfigurations. Operators who legitimately don't know where they want exports yet have two deliberate options post-fix: (1) set engagement_export_dir to a temp path now and update later; (2) hold off on commits until they decide. Either is a deliberate act; neither requires the system to invent a scratch location. The Open Engagement warning band (slice B affordance) is the right place to surface unconfigured engagements; it triggers at activation, before the operator's first write attempt. Investigation §E explicitly recommended this option.
title: Missing engagement_export_dir in meta DB: refuse the write

alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — Settings becomes engagement-aware directly.** Add Settings.active_engagement_code; make db_path and export_dir @property methods consulting meta DB based on that field. route_settings_to_engagement(code) becomes 'set Settings.active_engagement_code, reset caches.' Subprocess inheritance handled via either CRMBUILDER_V2_ACTIVE_ENGAGEMENT env var (Settings reads on init) or explicit CLI flag. Existing CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH / _EXPORT_DIR env vars become test-only or deprecated overrides. Rejected on diff-size grounds plus the observation that even option (b) ends up needing an env var or CLI flag for subprocess inheritance, so it's really option (a) plus a property-based facade.
consequences: Settings stays formally engagement-blind. Reading 'what engagement is active?' from code requires either reading current_engagement.json or inspecting env vars rather than asking Settings (slice B's affordances do this via resolve_active_engagement() the helper, not via Settings inspection). Tests that exercise routing manipulate env vars (with monkeypatch.setenv and cleanup) rather than constructing Settings with an engagement code. Some duplication of meta-DB-lookup logic between the route helper and the UI's existing ActiveEngagementContext — slice B's refactor of ActiveEngagementContext to use resolve_active_engagement() fixes this. The route helper's apply order at runtime is exact: meta DB lookup → env var set → reset_settings_cache → reset_engine_cache → reset_meta_engine_cache (conditional) → re-init pools.
context: The Settings-route fix from Bug 2 (DEC-109) requires Settings.export_dir to resolve per-engagement. Two architectural plumbing models are available: (a) env var + cache reset — the route helper sets os.environ['CRMBUILDER_V2_EXPORT_DIR'] and calls reset_settings_cache(), and Settings re-resolves on next get_settings() via the existing env_prefix mechanism that already wires CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH; (b) Settings becomes engagement-aware directly — add an active_engagement_code field to Settings, make db_path and export_dir computed @property methods consulting the meta DB based on that field. The env var pattern matches what's already in place for CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH; the direct-Settings pattern eliminates the env-var-roundtrip but requires a deeper refactor.
decision: **Env var + cache reset.** The new route_settings_to_engagement(code) helper reads the engagement record from the meta DB, computes per-engagement db_path via migration.lazy_migration.engagement_db_path, sets os.environ['CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH'] and os.environ['CRMBUILDER_V2_EXPORT_DIR'] (the latter to the actual path if non-null, else to the '__UNCONFIGURED__' sentinel per DEC-109), then calls reset_settings_cache() + reset_engine_cache() (+ reset_meta_engine_cache() when data_dir() would resolve differently). Next get_settings() re-resolves with the new env vars. Settings stays a plain config object. CRMBUILDER_V2_EXPORT_DIR is added to Settings as a sibling of CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH (same env_prefix mechanism, config.py:24) — the env var binding is the only Settings change required.
rationale: Smaller diff (~5 files vs ~10+ for the direct-Settings refactor) and avoids deepening the chicken-and-egg coupling between Settings.db_path → data_dir() → meta_db_path(). Subprocess inheritance is a forcing constraint: the desktop UI spawns the API as a subprocess and needs to communicate the active engagement across the process boundary; env vars are the natural carrier. Any 'Settings is engagement-aware' model still has to deal with the bootstrap question of how the API subprocess learns which engagement is active before Settings is first read — which lands back at 'an env var' or 'a CLI flag.' Option (b) ends up being option (a) plus a property-based facade, so the marginal benefit is small and the diff cost is much higher. Option (b) is the right shape for a v0.6+ architecture-cleanup workstream if Settings' role expands further; today, premature.
title: Plumbing model for engagement-aware Settings: env var + cache reset (matches existing CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH pattern)

alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — Defer to a follow-on workstream.** Ship the bug fix using env vars only; reword DEC-108's error to reference CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH=... uv run crmbuilder-v2-api instead of --engagement; file a small follow-on planning item for the flag. Rejected: the marginal cost is small, the flag's value compounds across every apply prompt from now on, and DEC-108's error message integrity is more important than minimizing slice-A surface area.
consequences: cli.py:run_api() gains an argparse step that today is parameter-less. Slight broadening of slice A's surface (~20 LOC + ~4 tests). The flag introduces a second way to do the same thing as the env var, which is normally a smell but is well-established in CLI tools (env var and CLI flag as equivalent override surfaces). The flag's documentation lives inline in `crmbuilder-v2-api --help`. From now on, apply-prompt pre-flights can use the cleaner `crmbuilder-v2-api --engagement CRMBUILDER &` form instead of the env-var prefix.
context: Pre-flight checks in apply prompts currently use the env-var-direct-set incantation: CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH=<path> uv run crmbuilder-v2-api &. This is awkward in shell pipelines and hard to remember. Investigation §G.5 noted that an explicit --engagement <code> CLI flag would let operators verify routing without manually setting env vars, and would support apply-prompt pre-flight checks more naturally. Additionally, DEC-108's fail-loud error message text references --engagement <code> as the standalone-launch remediation — so the flag's existence affects the honesty of that message. The question was whether to land the flag in slice A (alongside the helper) or defer to a follow-on workstream.
decision: **Land in slice A.** Add an argparse step in cli.py:run_api() that parses --engagement <code>, validates the code against the meta DB, and (if present) sets CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH via the route helper before reading Settings. Flag wins over marker when both are present — a yellow log line is emitted: '--engagement <flag> overrides current_engagement.json (<marker>)'. When flag matches the marker, no log line. Flag does NOT persist to current_engagement.json (it's an ephemeral override for one process). Unknown code → exit code 2 with message 'Unknown engagement \'<code>\'. Available: CRMBUILDER, CBM.' (enumerated from meta DB).
rationale: The cost is small (~20 LOC + happy-path + unknown-code + flag-overrides-marker tests — maybe 4 tests total). The flag makes DEC-108's error message honest as written (the error references the flag; without it the message points operators at something they can't actually do). The flag eliminates one operationally-awkward step from every apply-prompt pre-flight from now on. The flag is already in the workstream scope per Investigation §F. Deferring to a follow-on would mean either reworking DEC-108's error message now or shipping an error that points at a not-yet-existing flag.
title: Land --engagement <code> CLI flag on crmbuilder-v2-api in slice A

alternatives_considered: **Option (a) — Add /admin/runtime-info in slice A.** One endpoint returning a small diagnostic blob: active engagement code + name + engagement_export_dir + computed db_path; resolved Settings.db_path and Settings.export_dir; v2 version; data_dir. ~30 LOC + envelope wrapping + tests. **Option (b) — Add narrower /active-engagement in slice A.** Same idea but scoped to just the active engagement row, returning meta-DB fields verbatim. ~15 LOC. Both rejected on no-forcing-function grounds; the design surface is better designed once consumers have a real need.
consequences: Pre-flight prose stays slightly clunkier (filesystem read + curl rather than just curl). One follow-on PI to track if/when the need arises. Apply-prompt patterns continue working as they do today. Slice A's close-out documents the deferral in the slice plan's Deferred section.
context: Investigation §G.6 noted that the apply prompt assumed an /active-engagement endpoint exists for pre-flight verification; it doesn't. The OpenAPI surface only exposes /engagements and /engagements/{id}. Worth either adding /active-engagement or /admin/runtime-info to surface routing state without needing to read current_engagement.json directly. Marginal — the investigation explicitly framed it as 'quality-of-life follow-on, not load-bearing.' The question was whether to land it in slice A (symmetry with DEC-111's --engagement flag) or defer.
decision: **Defer entirely.** Pre-flights continue reading current_engagement.json directly (which is what they do today; the working pattern is established). File a follow-on planning item (PI-022 or whatever's next-available when the need actually arises) titled 'Diagnostic runtime-info API endpoint for ops verification' — not opened in this session, just noted as a future candidate. The slice A close-out adds a NOTE in the slice plan's Deferred section pointing at this decision.
rationale: Investigation flagged it as not load-bearing. Nothing in the bug fix needs it. Apply-prompts have a working pattern via filesystem reads of current_engagement.json. Adding an endpoint speculatively now means committing to a response shape before knowing what consumers want from it — better to design the schema when there's a real need driving it. DEC-111 (the --engagement CLI flag) is different because DEC-108's error message references the flag (forcing function); no forcing function here.
title: Defer /active-engagement and /admin/runtime-info API endpoints; file follow-on PI if real need arises

alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — Defensive per-site inline gates.** Each consumer rewrites its own check inline. Verbose; three places to update if the gate logic changes; no shared helper to maintain. Rejected on duplication grounds. **Option (c) — Active-path only, no helper.** Fix session_scope directly with inline gate logic; catalog exporter and force_export inherit Settings re-resolution (so they write to the right place when configured) but don't gate on the unconfigured sentinel; bootstrap/migrate untouched. Rejected because catalog exports are on the active path and inherit the same bug.
consequences: One small new helper function in runtime/engagement_routing.py (added alongside resolve_active_engagement and route_settings_to_engagement). Three single-line call sites at session_scope, force_export, and the catalog exporter. about_dialog explicitly does NOT gate; it grows display-side mapping in slice B for the sentinel and missing-path states. bootstrap/migrate.py outcome documented in the slice A commit message. The helper's responsibilities widened to carry both DEC-109's sentinel check and DEC-114's is_dir() check — the function is named assert_export_dir_ready(s) rather than assert_export_dir_configured(s) to reflect both responsibilities.
context: Investigation §G.1, §G.2, §G.3 listed three downstream consumers of Settings.export_dir besides session_scope: (1) catalog exporter at access/repositories/catalog/exports.py:57-59 — active path, has the same per-engagement-blindness as Bug 2; (2) force_export at access/db.py:120-140 — same shape as session_scope; not on any active production path today but inherits the same bug; (3) bootstrap/migrate.py:64 — uses settings.export_dir.parent; flagged as 'probably engine-scoped, but worth confirming.' The Settings-route fix from DEC-110 corrects what Settings.export_dir resolves to, but DEC-109's unconfigured-sentinel gate logic needs to live somewhere — either inline at each consumer site, or in a shared helper called from each site.
decision: **Centralized gate, applied to all active write paths via a shared assert_export_dir_ready(s: Settings) -> None helper.** The helper lives in crmbuilder_v2.runtime.engagement_routing (the new module from DEC-110), checks for the DEC-109 sentinel and the DEC-114 missing-path condition, raises EngagementExportDirNotConfigured or EngagementExportDirMissing per case, or returns None silently when ready. Call sites: session_scope (before write_staging), force_export (before write_staging), catalog exporter's path-return site. about_dialog reads Settings.export_dir raw with display-side mapping for the sentinel and missing-path states (no gate — it's a display, not a write). bootstrap/migrate.py:64 gets a brief code-read pass during slice A; if engine-scoped, hardcode the path and remove the settings.export_dir.parent indirection; if engagement-scoped, apply the gate.
rationale: Today's export_dir bug is one class of bug with four manifestations. If we only gate session_scope, we leave three latent regressions in the codebase that surface the moment someone exercises catalog exports under an unconfigured engagement, or someone wires force_export into a Recovery path. Cost of the centralized helper is one new function (~10 LOC) plus three call sites (one line each) — strictly less than the cost of three separate inline gates that duplicate the same check logic. Catalog exports specifically MUST be fixed because they're on the active path (Investigation §G.1 was explicit). force_export is free to add since the helper exists. bootstrap/migrate gets a 10-minute code-read and a one-line fix per branch.
title: Centralized export-write gate applied to all active write paths via assert_export_dir_ready helper

alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — Continue auto-creating, full depth.** Match today's behavior; mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True) runs on the full path; silent on missing trees. Rejected because today's behavior is the bug §G.7 flagged. **Option (c) — Auto-create leaf only.** Check parent of engagement_export_dir exists; if it does, auto-create the leaf if missing; if parent doesn't exist, fail loud. Rejected as over-engineered — the parent-vs-leaf rule is one more thing operators have to internalize; clean fail-loud is simpler.
consequences: Operator workflow gains a one-time setup step per engagement: create the engagement_export_dir on disk before (or after) configuring it in Edit Engagement. Edge case: a fresh client repo where the methodology-records subtree exists but db-export/ hasn't been created yet — operators mkdir db-export/ once, then writes work forever. The exception name is EngagementExportDirMissing (sibling to DEC-109's EngagementExportDirNotConfigured under a shared EngagementExportDirError base for catchability). Slice B's Edit Engagement dialog grows save-time validation that prompts on non-existent paths with [Save anyway] / [Cancel]. Slice B's Open Engagement flow grows a red warning band when the active engagement's export_dir is set but missing on disk. about_dialog's display in slice B renders '(missing — <path>)' for this state.
context: Investigation §G.7 flagged that access/exporter.py:115 calls export_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True) before writing — today's behavior of silently writing to the wrong location is doubly insidious: it not only writes to the wrong place, it auto-creates the wrong directory tree if needed. The Settings-route fix corrects the routing logic but leaves silent-mkdir-on-typo behavior in place. Scenario: operator configures CBM's engagement_export_dir to /home/doug/Dropbox/Projects/ClevelandBusinessMentors/PRDs/methodology-records/db-export, later reorganizes Dropbox and moves the local CBM clone to /home/doug/Repos/ClevelandBusinessMentors/, forgets to update the engagement record. Today: write_staging silently creates an empty Dropbox tree mirroring the old structure; CBM's actual repo gets nothing. The question: fail-loud if engagement_export_dir doesn't exist on disk, or auto-create?
decision: **Fail loud if engagement_export_dir doesn't exist on disk.** The gate helper assert_export_dir_ready(s) (from DEC-113) checks export_dir.is_dir() before write. If False, raises EngagementExportDirMissing (new exception, sibling to DEC-109's EngagementExportDirNotConfigured). Message: 'Configured engagement_export_dir does not exist on disk: <path>. Either create the directory or update the engagement via Edit Engagement.' Subdirectories below the configured root (catalog/, snapshots/, future structure) continue to be auto-created with mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True) — that's the system's space to manage. The configured root is the operator's choice; the system doesn't invent or create it on demand.
rationale: Aligns with the philosophy of DEC-108, DEC-109, DEC-113: no silent fallbacks, no silent rogue-tree creation. The configured engagement_export_dir is supposed to point at an actual existing directory in a real client repo — it's a target operators chose, not a target the system invents. Operator workflow becomes 'create the engagement_export_dir on disk first, then configure it in Edit Engagement.' One-time setup cost per engagement, not per-write. The Edit Engagement dialog (slice B affordance) can grow a 'Create directory' button or validate-at-save in a future polish slice if the manual-mkdir step becomes friction.
title: Fail loud if engagement_export_dir doesn't exist on disk; do not auto-create the configured root

alternatives_considered: (a) Keep kill-and-relaunch but force-kill the API — impossible for an externally-launched process the UI does not own, which is the documented dev workflow. (b) Reconcile routing to current_engagement.json on every request — mutates global process state (env, caches, pools) under uvicorn's threadpool; hazardous under concurrency. (c) In-process re-route via an explicit endpoint (chosen) — a single controlled call during activation while the UI is blocked by the overlay, no concurrency hazard.
consequences: New /admin router. Activation worker keeps a spawn fallback for the down-API case. The UI process's own get_settings() is still only routed at startup, so About-dialog db_path can lag after a switch — Connection Info (DEC-116) queries the API for authoritative state instead. multi-engagement-architecture.md §4 is now stale and should be updated to describe the re-route.
context: multi-engagement-architecture.md §4 specified switching engagements by killing the API subprocess and relaunching it bound to the new engagement's DB (the DB is fixed at spawn via CRMBUILDER_V2_DB_PATH + lru-cached Settings). This silently failed against an externally-launched API ('crmbuilder-v2-api &'): ServerLifecycle.terminate() is a no-op for a process the UI does not own, and start() short-circuits when /health already responds, so the switch re-attached to the old process on the old DB. Displayed data never changed — the reported bug.
decision: Switch by re-routing the live API in place. New POST /admin/active-engagement calls route_settings_to_engagement (DEC-110) to reset the settings + engine caches against the target DB; no subprocess restart. The activation worker's build_lifecycle_managers takes the StorageClient; kill_api is a no-op when the client is wired; launch_api POSTs the re-route and falls back to the legacy spawn only when the API is unreachable. Works identically for a UI-owned or an externally-launched API.
rationale: Re-routing reuses the in-process routing helper DEC-110 already built for exactly this ('in-process re-routing under a long-running UI'). It works regardless of API ownership — the UI cannot kill a process it does not own, so kill-and-relaunch could never fix the external case. It also removes the port-release race between terminate and respawn and is faster (no process start). The spawn fallback preserves recovery when the API is genuinely down.
title: Engagement switching re-routes the live API in-process, not via kill-and-relaunch

alternatives_considered: (a) One opaque /admin/runtime-info blob as DEC-112 sketched — split into /connection vs /version for clearer semantics and API docs. (b) Maintain a separate human-readable schema_version constant — redundant with and prone to drifting from the Alembic revisions. (c) Read versions UI-side from get_settings() — stale after a switch; rejected in favor of an API query.
consequences: DEC-112's deferral is discharged; no follow-on PI for the diagnostic endpoint is needed. Future schema migrations automatically surface as 'migration pending' in the Connection Info dialog until applied. The endpoints are general-purpose operator/diagnostic surfaces other tooling can consume.
context: DEC-112 deferred a diagnostic API endpoint (/admin/runtime-info) with 'file a follow-on when a real need arises.' Two needs arrived together: diagnosing the engagement-switch bug (DEC-115) required knowing which DB the live API was actually bound to, and the operator asked to verify which API and database versions are running. There was also no single source of truth for the API version (FastAPI app version was a hardcoded '0.1.0' diverged from __version__ = 0.6.0) and no surfaced notion of a database version.
decision: Add GET /admin/connection (bound DB path / exists / size, engagement code derived from the DB filename, export-dir state) and GET /admin/version (api_version from crmbuilder_v2.__version__; the active-engagement DB and meta DB Alembic schema versions as current-revision vs chain-head plus an up_to_date flag). Source the FastAPI app + root version from __version__. Add migration/version_info.py to read each DB's stamped revision and chain head. Add a Help → Connection Info… dialog that queries the API so it reflects live server state.
rationale: The database version system already exists — it is the Alembic migration chain, whose revisions are cleanly numbered (0001..0010). Surfacing current-vs-head is the natural 'what version is this DB' answer and flags pending migrations for free. The API version is single-sourced from __version__. Querying the API (not the UI's get_settings(), which only routes at startup) makes the dialog authoritative after an in-process switch.
title: Build /admin/connection + /admin/version introspection and a DB/API version surface (the diagnostic endpoint DEC-112 deferred)

alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — One generalized governed-artifact entity type.** A single entity type with a `kind` discriminator field (eleven values: workstream_plan, methodology_guide, schema_specification, prd, implementation_plan, session_startup_document, apply_script, kickoff_prompt, slice_execution_prompt, close_out_payload, deposit_event) and a status enum supporting the union of all lifecycles. Rejected on schema-complexity grounds — the optional-field count and the per-kind validation logic exceeds the cost of three smaller entities. **Option (c) — A conversation entity ties everything together, and artifacts are subsumed under it.** Rejected because conversations and artifacts are different governance objects; the conversation question is settled separately by DEC-119.
consequences: Three new entity types must be designed in the governance schema-design workstream: reference book, work ticket, deposit bucket. Each gets its own per-entity schema-design conversation. The deposit bucket family expands to two entities (close-out payload and deposit event) per DEC-118, raising the workstream's per-entity-schema conversation count from three to four for the file-tracking side, plus two more (conversation, workstream) for the unit-of-work side, totalling six per-entity schema-design conversations. The conventions inherited from the methodology entity schema-design workstream — parent-prefix field naming (DEC-046), source-first relationship-kind naming (DEC-048), three-status lifecycle posture — apply to all six new entity types where compatible. New relationship kinds in the references vocabulary must be added per Decision 006's universal-references-table pattern and per the strict reference-vocab discipline established since.
context: The V2 governance database does not track any of the operational artifacts the planning-and-execution workflow produces or consumes (kickoff prompts, workstream master plans, Product Requirements Documents, implementation plans, methodology guides, schema specifications, per-slice execution prompts, close-out payloads, apply scripts, the session-startup entry document). These ten artifact types are governance-meaningful — each can fail to exist, exist in stale state, be superseded, or be in flight — but none is queryable from the database. The gap directly contradicts the stated single-source-of-truth role. The first decision was how to structure the database additions: one generalized entity-type with a discriminator field accommodating all ten kinds, or multiple purpose-built entity types each modeling a distinct lifecycle.
decision: **Three purpose-built entity-type families, one per lifecycle shape.** Family 1 (reference book) covers long-lived multi-consumer versioned reference documents — seven of the ten artifacts fit here (workstream master plan, methodology guide, schema specification, Product Requirements Document, implementation plan, session-startup entry document, apply script). Family 2 (work ticket) covers single-consumer seed documents — two of the ten fit here (kickoff prompt, per-slice execution prompt). Family 3 (deposit bucket) covers single-use state-write packages — one artifact fits here (close-out payload), expanded by DEC-118 to also include the apply event as a separate but paired record. Each family becomes its own entity type with a schema fitted to its lifecycle.
rationale: The three families have meaningfully different lifecycles. Family 1 has versioning and supersession with many readers across time. Family 2 has produce-and-consume-once semantics with at most one consumer. Family 3 has produce-and-apply-once semantics with a verification linkage to the records its application wrote. A single generalized governed-artifact entity would need to carry the union of all three lifecycles in one schema — optional fields per family, a status enum accommodating all transitions, conditional validation by kind. The cost of three smaller purpose-fitted schemas is strictly less than the cost of one large general schema with three modes lurking inside it, and the per-family schemas are easier to reason about per type at design time and at use time.
title: Track workflow files as three purpose-built entity-type families, not one generalized governed-artifact type

alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — One combined deposit entity with optional apply-side fields.** Payload fields populated at conversation close; outcome and resulting-record-list populated at apply time. Status enum spans both phases (drafted, applied_success, applied_partial, applied_failure). Rejected because the two phases have meaningfully different timing and immutability semantics — payloads can in principle be revised before apply; deposit events are immutable transactional records.
consequences: Six entity types in the governance schema-design workstream total, not five: workstream, conversation, reference book, work ticket, close-out payload, deposit event. A new relationship kind in the references vocabulary expresses payload-to-deposit-event linkage (provisional name `applied_in`, naming finalized at the close-out-payload schema-design conversation). The deposit event entity carries linkages to (a) the close-out payload it applied (one-to-one or possibly one-to-zero-or-one if a payload is authored but never applied), (b) the apply script version that ran it (a reference book record), (c) the conversation whose close produced the payload (a conversation record). The apply outcome enum captures success / partial / failure with sub-fields for partial-failure detail. The resulting-record-list captures the actual identifiers written so 'what did this apply produce' is queryable directly.
context: DEC-117 establishes the deposit bucket as one of three artifact-tracking families. The initial framing of the family had it as one entity holding both the structured payload (the file produced at conversation close listing what should be written) and the act of applying it (the record of when the apply ran with what outcome). Doug's question during the scoping discussion — 'how do we know if the script/payload met the requirements if they are unavailable to a user using the DB?' — forced the question of whether one entity is enough.
decision: **Two entities within the deposit bucket family.** The close-out payload entity captures the structured package produced at conversation close — the slip declaring what should be written. The deposit event entity captures the application of a payload — the deposit, linking the payload to its application outcome (success, partial, failure) and to the identifiers of the records the apply wrote (session, decisions, planning items, references). The deposit event is a transactional record, not a document, and its lifecycle differs from the payload's: a payload is produced once at conversation close and applied (typically) once; a deposit event is created at apply-time and immutable thereafter.
rationale: The verification question Doug raised is a governance question, not an implementation question. To answer 'did the apply do what the planning conversation said it should,' the database needs both the payload (the spec) and a record of the apply (the outcome) as separate first-class records. A single combined entity cannot represent both clearly because the lifecycles are out of phase — the payload exists from the moment it is authored; the deposit event does not exist until the apply runs. A single entity would need optional 'application-side' fields that may or may not be populated, plus a status field whose meaning straddles the two phases. Two entities with a relationship between them keeps each schema clean and lets verification queries express themselves directly.
title: Within the deposit bucket family, separate the close-out payload from the deposit event as two entities, not one

alternatives_considered: **Option (b) — Allow sessions to be created in a 'planned' state and transitioned to 'complete' at close.** Rejected because it violates DEC-013's append-only rule. Sessions are records of fact; the work-unit state lives elsewhere. **Option (c) — Use planning items to track pending conversations.** Rejected because planning items have different semantics (deferred design questions, open issues), and conflating them with planned conversations would muddy both concepts.
consequences: A new entity type to design in the schema-design workstream. The conversation entity is the second in the per-entity sequence per the workstream master plan (after workstream, which it references). A new relationship kind for the conversation-to-session linkage. A new relationship kind for predecessor and successor chains between conversations. The work-ticket entity (DEC-117) needs a relationship to the conversation entity (one work ticket is for one conversation). The close-out-payload entity (DEC-117 / DEC-118) needs a relationship to the conversation entity (one payload is produced by one conversation). Retroactive question: backfill conversation records for sessions already in the database (the original 45 sessions from SES-001 through SES-045), or only go forward? Captured as PI-022 in this close-out for resolution later.
context: The V2 governance database tracks sessions, which are after-the-fact records of conversations that have happened. Sessions are append-only per DEC-013. The database has no concept of a conversation that is planned but not yet opened, a conversation whose kickoff prompt is drafted but not yet executed, or a conversation that is in flight. Queries like 'what is queued up next' have no answer in the database because the planned-but-not-yet-happened state is not modeled. The work-ticket entity (DEC-117) covers kickoff prompts but not the unit of conversational work itself — a conversation may have no kickoff prompt (the workstream-establishing conversation for the methodology workstream had none), and a kickoff prompt may be superseded before being used.
decision: **Add a conversation entity representing the unit of conversational work through its full lifecycle.** Statuses: planned, kickoff_drafted, ready, in_flight, complete, cancelled, superseded. Fields (subject to the conversation entity's schema-design conversation) include identifier (Conversation NNN), purpose, position-in-workstream, optional kickoff-prompt reference (to a work-ticket record), predecessor-conversation reference, successor-conversation reference, and workstream membership. The session record continues to exist as the after-the-fact record per DEC-013's append-only rule; the conversation record links to the session via a new relationship kind (provisional name `recorded_as`, naming finalized at the conversation entity's schema-design conversation) once the conversation completes.
rationale: The session entity captures what happened. The conversation entity captures the unit of work. These are different governance objects. A conversation may exist as a planned work item before any session record exists; a session record exists only at the moment of close.
